Example 2: Find and Delete
#!/bin/bash
# Find UUID and delete
FUNCTION_NAME="anihpj-old-processor"

UUID=$(aws lambda list-event-source-mappings \
  --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
  --query 'EventSourceMappings[0].UUID' \
  --output text)

aws lambda delete-event-source-mapping --uuid $UUID
echo "✓ Deleted mapping: $UUID"
✓ Deleted mapping: a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890
# Find and delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Found and deleted mapping")
Example 3: Delete All Function Mappings
#!/bin/bash
# Remove all mappings for function
FUNCTION_NAME="anihpj-retiring-processor"

for UUID in $(aws lambda list-event-source-mappings \
  --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
  --query 'EventSourceMappings[*].UUID' \
  --output text); do
  aws lambda delete-event-source-mapping --uuid $UUID
  echo "Deleted: $UUID"
done
Deleted: a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890 Deleted: b2c3d4e5-f6a7-8901-bcde-f23456789012
# Delete all mappings from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost deleted_count = 2 print(f"Deleted {deleted_count} mappings")
Example 4: Delete Disabled Mappings
#!/bin/bash
# Cleanup disabled mappings
for UUID in $(aws lambda list-event-source-mappings \
  --query 'EventSourceMappings[?State==`Disabled`].UUID' \
  --output text); do
  aws lambda delete-event-source-mapping --uuid $UUID
  echo "Removed disabled: $UUID"
done
Removed disabled: x1y2z3a4-b5c6-7890-defg-h12345678901
# Delete disabled from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Cleaned up disabled mappings")
Example 5: Safe Delete with Confirmation
#!/bin/bash
# Confirm before deleting
UUID="a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890"

read -p "Delete mapping $UUID? (y/n): " confirm
if [ "$confirm" = "y" ]; then
  aws lambda delete-event-source-mapping --uuid $UUID
  echo "✓ Deleted"
else
  echo "Cancelled"
fi
Delete mapping a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890? (y/n): y ✓ Deleted
# Safe delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"User confirmed deletion")

publish-version

Creates an immutable version from the current function code and configuration. Versions enable rollbacks, blue-green deployments, and alias-based routing for Django Lambda functions.

Syntax
aws lambda publish-version \
  --function-name  \
  [--code-sha256 ] \
  [--description ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name or ARN
  • --code-sha256 (string): Expected code hash (validation)
  • --description (string): Version description
Return Value
  • Version (String): Published version number
  • FunctionArn (String): Version-qualified ARN
  • CodeSha256 (String): Code hash
Example 1: Publish Version
#!/bin/bash
# Publish new version
aws lambda publish-version \
  --function-name anihpj-email-sender \
  --description "v1.2.0 - Added template support" \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Version published"
{ "FunctionName": "anihpj-email-sender", "Version": "5", "Description": "v1.2.0 - Added template support", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-email-sender:5" } ✓ Version published
# Publish version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost version = {'number': '5', 'description': 'v1.2.0 - Added template support'} print(f"Published: Version {version['number']}")
Example 2: Publish with SHA Validation
#!/bin/bash
# Ensure correct code is published
CODE_SHA="abc123def456789..."

aws lambda publish-version \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --code-sha256 $CODE_SHA \
  --description "Release 2.0.0" \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Version": "10", "CodeSha256": "abc123def456789...", "Description": "Release 2.0.0" }
# Publish with validation from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Code SHA validated before publish")
Example 3: CI/CD Version Publish
#!/bin/bash
# Automated version publish
FUNCTION_NAME="anihpj-image-processor"
GIT_TAG=$(git describe --tags)
GIT_COMMIT=$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)

VERSION=$(aws lambda publish-version \
  --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
  --description "$GIT_TAG ($GIT_COMMIT)" \
  --query 'Version' \
  --output text)

echo "✓ Published version: $VERSION"
✓ Published version: 15
# CI/CD publish from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version 15 published from CI/CD")
Example 4: Get Version ARN
#!/bin/bash
# Publish and get full ARN
VERSION_ARN=$(aws lambda publish-version \
  --function-name anihpj-report-generator \
  --description "Monthly report update" \
  --query 'FunctionArn' \
  --output text)

echo "Version ARN: $VERSION_ARN"
Version ARN: arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-report-generator:8
# Get version ARN from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost arn = "arn:aws:lambda:...:anihpj-report-generator:8" print(f"Version ARN ready for alias")
Example 5: Publish After Code Update
#!/bin/bash
# Update code and publish
FUNCTION_NAME="anihpj-queue-processor"

# Update code
aws lambda update-function-code \
  --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
  --zip-file fileb://deployment.zip

# Wait for update
aws lambda wait function-updated --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME

# Publish version
aws lambda publish-version \
  --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
  --description "Post-update version"

echo "✓ Code updated and version published"
{ "Version": "12", "LastModified": "2026-01-13T14:00:00.000+0000" } ✓ Code updated and version published
# Update and publish from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Code updated → Version 12 published")

list-versions-by-function

Lists all published versions of a Lambda function. View version history, find rollback candidates, and track Django function evolution over time.

Syntax
aws lambda list-versions-by-function \
  --function-name  \
  [--max-items ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name or ARN
  • --max-items (integer): Maximum versions to return
Return Value
  • Versions (List): Array of version configurations
  • NextMarker (String): Pagination token
Example 1: List All Versions
#!/bin/bash
# List all function versions
aws lambda list-versions-by-function \
  --function-name anihpj-email-sender \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Versions": [ {"Version": "$LATEST", "Description": ""}, {"Version": "1", "Description": "Initial release"}, {"Version": "2", "Description": "Bug fix"}, {"Version": "3", "Description": "v1.1.0"}, {"Version": "4", "Description": "v1.2.0"}, {"Version": "5", "Description": "v1.2.0 - Added template support"} ] }
# List versions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost versions = ['$LATEST', '1', '2', '3', '4', '5'] print(f"Found {len(versions)} versions")
Example 2: Table Format
#!/bin/bash
# Display versions as table
aws lambda list-versions-by-function \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --query 'Versions[*].{Version:Version,Description:Description,Modified:LastModified}' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1
------------------------------------------------------------- | ListVersionsByFunction | +---------+---------------------------+---------------------+ | Version | Description | Modified | +---------+---------------------------+---------------------+ | $LATEST | | 2026-01-13T14:00:00 | | 10 | Release 2.0.0 | 2026-01-10T10:00:00 | | 9 | Bug fix | 2026-01-05T09:00:00 | +---------+---------------------------+---------------------+
# Version table from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version history displayed")
Example 3: Get Latest Version Number
#!/bin/bash
# Get highest version number
LATEST_VERSION=$(aws lambda list-versions-by-function \
  --function-name anihpj-image-processor \
  --query 'Versions[-1].Version' \
  --output text)

echo "Latest published version: $LATEST_VERSION"
Latest published version: 15
# Latest version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost latest = 15 print(f"Latest version: {latest}")
Example 4: Count Versions
#!/bin/bash
# Count published versions
VERSION_COUNT=$(aws lambda list-versions-by-function \
  --function-name anihpj-report-generator \
  --query 'length(Versions[?Version!=`$LATEST`])' \
  --output text)

echo "Total published versions: $VERSION_COUNT"
Total published versions: 8
# Count versions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost count = 8 print(f"Published versions: {count}")
Example 5: Find Version by Description
#!/bin/bash
# Search for specific version
aws lambda list-versions-by-function \
  --function-name anihpj-queue-processor \
  --query 'Versions[?contains(Description,`v1.1`)].{Version:Version,Desc:Description}' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1
--------------------------------- | ListVersionsByFunction | +---------+---------------------+ | Version | Desc | +---------+---------------------+ | 3 | v1.1.0 - Initial | | 4 | v1.1.1 - Hotfix | +---------+---------------------+
# Search versions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost matches = [{'version': '3'}, {'version': '4'}] print(f"Found {len(matches)} matching versions")

create-alias

Creates an alias that points to a Lambda function version. Aliases enable blue-green deployments, canary releases, and environment separation (dev, staging, prod) for Django functions.

Syntax
aws lambda create-alias \
  --function-name  \
  --name  \
  --function-version  \
  [--description ] \
  [--routing-config AdditionalVersionWeights={=}] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name
  • --name (string): [Required] Alias name (dev, staging, prod)
  • --function-version (string): [Required] Version to point to
  • --description (string): Alias description
  • --routing-config (structure): Traffic splitting for canary
Return Value
  • AliasArn (String): Alias ARN
  • Name (String): Alias name
  • FunctionVersion (String): Target version
Example 1: Create Production Alias
#!/bin/bash
# Create prod alias pointing to stable version
aws lambda create-alias \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --name prod \
  --function-version 10 \
  --description "Production environment" \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Prod alias created"
{ "AliasArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-api-handler:prod", "Name": "prod", "FunctionVersion": "10", "Description": "Production environment" } ✓ Prod alias created
# Create prod alias from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost alias = {'name': 'prod', 'version': '10'} print(f"Alias {alias['name']} → v{alias['version']}")
Example 2: Create Staging Alias
#!/bin/bash
# Create staging for testing
aws lambda create-alias \
  --function-name anihpj-email-sender \
  --name staging \
  --function-version 5 \
  --description "Staging environment for QA" \
  --region us-east-1
{ "AliasArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-email-sender:staging", "Name": "staging", "FunctionVersion": "5" }
# Create staging from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Staging alias created for QA")
Example 3: Create Dev Alias ($LATEST)
#!/bin/bash
# Dev alias points to $LATEST
aws lambda create-alias \
  --function-name anihpj-image-processor \
  --name dev \
  --function-version '$LATEST' \
  --description "Development - always latest code" \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Name": "dev", "FunctionVersion": "$LATEST", "Description": "Development - always latest code" }
# Dev alias from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Dev alias → $LATEST (auto-updates)")
Example 4: Canary Deployment (Traffic Split)
#!/bin/bash
# 90% to v10, 10% to v11 (canary)
aws lambda create-alias \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --name canary \
  --function-version 10 \
  --routing-config 'AdditionalVersionWeights={"11":0.1}' \
  --description "Canary: 90% v10, 10% v11" \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Name": "canary", "FunctionVersion": "10", "RoutingConfig": { "AdditionalVersionWeights": {"11": 0.1} } }
# Canary deployment from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Canary: 90% v10, 10% v11") print(f"✓ Gradual rollout enabled")
Example 5: Blue-Green Setup
#!/bin/bash
# Create blue and green aliases
FUNCTION="anihpj-queue-processor"

aws lambda create-alias \
  --function-name $FUNCTION \
  --name blue \
  --function-version 8 \
  --description "Blue (current production)"

aws lambda create-alias \
  --function-name $FUNCTION \
  --name green \
  --function-version 9 \
  --description "Green (new release candidate)"

echo "✓ Blue-Green aliases created"
{ "Name": "blue", "FunctionVersion": "8" } { "Name": "green", "FunctionVersion": "9" } ✓ Blue-Green aliases created
# Blue-Green setup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Blue (v8) = Production") print(f"Green (v9) = New release")

update-alias

Updates a Lambda function alias to point to a different version. Promotes new versions to production, adjusts canary weights, or performs instant rollbacks for Django functions.

Syntax
aws lambda update-alias \
  --function-name  \
  --name  \
  [--function-version ] \
  [--description ] \
  [--routing-config AdditionalVersionWeights={=}] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name
  • --name (string): [Required] Alias name to update
  • --function-version (string): New target version
  • --routing-config (structure): Updated traffic weights
Return Value
  • Name (String): Alias name
  • FunctionVersion (String): New target version
  • RoutingConfig (Object): Updated routing
Example 1: Promote to Production
#!/bin/bash
# Promote new version to prod
aws lambda update-alias \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --name prod \
  --function-version 11 \
  --description "Promoted v11 to production" \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ v11 now in production"
{ "Name": "prod", "FunctionVersion": "11", "Description": "Promoted v11 to production" } ✓ v11 now in production
# Promote to prod from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Prod alias: v10 → v11") print(f"✓ Instant deployment")
Example 2: Instant Rollback
#!/bin/bash
# Rollback prod to previous version
aws lambda update-alias \
  --function-name anihpj-email-sender \
  --name prod \
  --function-version 4 \
  --description "Rollback to v4 - v5 had issues" \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Rolled back to v4"
{ "Name": "prod", "FunctionVersion": "4", "Description": "Rollback to v4 - v5 had issues" } ✓ Rolled back to v4
# Rollback from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"ROLLBACK: v5 → v4") print(f"✓ Previous version restored")
Example 3: Increase Canary Traffic
#!/bin/bash
# Increase new version traffic to 30%
aws lambda update-alias \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --name prod \
  --function-version 10 \
  --routing-config 'AdditionalVersionWeights={"11":0.3}' \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Canary: 70% v10, 30% v11"
{ "Name": "prod", "FunctionVersion": "10", "RoutingConfig": { "AdditionalVersionWeights": {"11": 0.3} } } ✓ Canary: 70% v10, 30% v11
# Increase canary from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Traffic split: 70/30") print(f"✓ Gradual rollout progressing")
Example 4: Complete Canary Promotion
#!/bin/bash
# Canary successful - promote fully
aws lambda update-alias \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --name prod \
  --function-version 11 \
  --routing-config 'AdditionalVersionWeights={}' \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ v11 now receiving 100% traffic"
{ "Name": "prod", "FunctionVersion": "11", "RoutingConfig": { "AdditionalVersionWeights": {} } } ✓ v11 now receiving 100% traffic
# Complete promotion from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Canary complete: 100% on v11")
Example 5: Blue-Green Swap
#!/bin/bash
# Swap blue and green
FUNCTION="anihpj-queue-processor"

# Get current versions
BLUE_VER=$(aws lambda get-alias --function-name $FUNCTION --name blue --query 'FunctionVersion' --output text)
GREEN_VER=$(aws lambda get-alias --function-name $FUNCTION --name green --query 'FunctionVersion' --output text)

# Swap
aws lambda update-alias --function-name $FUNCTION --name blue --function-version $GREEN_VER
aws lambda update-alias --function-name $FUNCTION --name green --function-version $BLUE_VER

echo "✓ Blue-Green swapped"
{ "Name": "blue", "FunctionVersion": "9" } { "Name": "green", "FunctionVersion": "8" } ✓ Blue-Green swapped
# Blue-Green swap from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Blue: v8 → v9") print(f"Green: v9 → v8")

list-aliases

Lists all aliases for a Lambda function. View environment mappings (dev, staging, prod), routing configurations, and version associations for Django functions.

Syntax
aws lambda list-aliases \
  --function-name  \
  [--function-version ] \
  [--max-items ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name
  • --function-version (string): Filter by version
  • --max-items (integer): Maximum aliases to return
Return Value
  • Aliases (List): Array of alias configurations
Example 1: List All Aliases
#!/bin/bash
# List all function aliases
aws lambda list-aliases \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Aliases": [ {"Name": "dev", "FunctionVersion": "$LATEST", "Description": "Development"}, {"Name": "staging", "FunctionVersion": "10", "Description": "Staging QA"}, {"Name": "prod", "FunctionVersion": "9", "Description": "Production"} ] }
# List aliases from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost aliases = ['dev', 'staging', 'prod'] print(f"Found {len(aliases)} aliases")
Example 2: Table Format
#!/bin/bash
# Display as table
aws lambda list-aliases \
  --function-name anihpj-email-sender \
  --query 'Aliases[*].{Name:Name,Version:FunctionVersion,Description:Description}' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1
---------------------------------------------- | ListAliases | +---------+---------+------------------------+ | Name | Version | Description | +---------+---------+------------------------+ | dev | $LATEST | Development | | staging | 5 | Staging QA | | prod | 4 | Production | +---------+---------+------------------------+
# Alias table from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment → Version mapping")
Example 3: Find Aliases for Version
#!/bin/bash
# Which aliases point to version 10?
aws lambda list-aliases \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --function-version 10 \
  --query 'Aliases[*].Name' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1
staging canary
# Aliases for version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost aliases_v10 = ['staging', 'canary'] print(f"v10 used by: {aliases_v10}")
Example 4: Show Routing Config
#!/bin/bash
# View traffic split configurations
aws lambda list-aliases \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --query 'Aliases[*].{Name:Name,Version:FunctionVersion,Routing:RoutingConfig}' \
  --region us-east-1
[ { "Name": "prod", "Version": "10", "Routing": {"AdditionalVersionWeights": {"11": 0.1}} }, { "Name": "staging", "Version": "11", "Routing": null } ]
# Routing config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Prod: 90% v10, 10% v11 (canary)")
Example 5: Export Alias Inventory
#!/bin/bash
# Export for documentation
aws lambda list-aliases \
  --function-name anihpj-image-processor \
  --output json \
  --region us-east-1 > aliases.json

cat aliases.json | jq '.Aliases[] | "\(.Name) → v\(.FunctionVersion)"'
"dev → v$LATEST" "staging → v14" "prod → v13"
# Export aliases from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Alias inventory exported to JSON")

delete-alias

Deletes a Lambda function alias. Remove deprecated environment aliases or cleanup after blue-green deployments. Does not delete the function or versions.

Syntax
aws lambda delete-alias \
  --function-name  \
  --name  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name
  • --name (string): [Required] Alias name to delete
Return Value
  • No response body on success (HTTP 204)
Example 1: Delete Deprecated Alias
#!/bin/bash
# Remove old alias
aws lambda delete-alias \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --name old-prod \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Alias deleted"
✓ Alias deleted
# Delete alias from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Alias old-prod deleted")
Example 2: Delete Canary Alias
#!/bin/bash
# Remove canary after full promotion
aws lambda delete-alias \
  --function-name anihpj-email-sender \
  --name canary \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Canary alias removed"
✓ Canary alias removed
# Delete canary from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Canary removed after promotion")
Example 3: Cleanup Test Aliases
#!/bin/bash
# Remove all test aliases
FUNCTION="anihpj-image-processor"

for ALIAS in $(aws lambda list-aliases \
  --function-name $FUNCTION \
  --query 'Aliases[?contains(Name,`test`)].Name' \
  --output text); do
  aws lambda delete-alias --function-name $FUNCTION --name $ALIAS
  echo "Deleted: $ALIAS"
done
Deleted: test-v1 Deleted: test-v2
# Cleanup test aliases from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost deleted = ['test-v1', 'test-v2'] print(f"Deleted {len(deleted)} test aliases")
Example 4: Delete with Confirmation
#!/bin/bash
# Safe delete with prompt
FUNCTION="anihpj-report-generator"
ALIAS="staging"

read -p "Delete alias $ALIAS? (y/n): " confirm
if [ "$confirm" = "y" ]; then
  aws lambda delete-alias --function-name $FUNCTION --name $ALIAS
  echo "✓ Deleted"
fi
Delete alias staging? (y/n): y ✓ Deleted
# Safe delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"User confirmed deletion")
Example 5: Blue-Green Cleanup
#!/bin/bash
# Remove blue-green aliases after merge
FUNCTION="anihpj-queue-processor"

aws lambda delete-alias --function-name $FUNCTION --name blue
aws lambda delete-alias --function-name $FUNCTION --name green

echo "✓ Blue-green aliases removed"
✓ Blue-green aliases removed
# Blue-green cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Blue-green cleanup complete")

put-function-concurrency

Sets reserved concurrency for a Lambda function. Reserve capacity for critical Django tasks or limit concurrency to prevent downstream service overload (database connections, API rate limits).

Syntax
aws lambda put-function-concurrency \
  --function-name  \
  --reserved-concurrent-executions  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name
  • --reserved-concurrent-executions (integer): [Required] Reserved instances (0 to disable, 1+ to reserve)
Return Value
  • ReservedConcurrentExecutions (Integer): Reserved capacity
Example 1: Reserve Concurrency for Critical Function
#!/bin/bash
# Reserve 100 instances for email sender
aws lambda put-function-concurrency \
  --function-name anihpj-email-sender \
  --reserved-concurrent-executions 100 \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Reserved 100 concurrent executions"
{ "ReservedConcurrentExecutions": 100 } ✓ Reserved 100 concurrent executions
# Reserve concurrency from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Email sender: 100 reserved instances") print(f"✓ Guaranteed capacity")
Example 2: Limit Database Connections
#!/bin/bash
# Limit to prevent DB connection exhaustion
aws lambda put-function-concurrency \
  --function-name anihpj-db-sync \
  --reserved-concurrent-executions 10 \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Limited to 10 concurrent (protects DB)"
{ "ReservedConcurrentExecutions": 10 } ✓ Limited to 10 concurrent (protects DB)
# Limit for DB protection from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Max 10 concurrent DB connections") print(f"✓ Database protected")
Example 3: Disable Function (Set to 0)
#!/bin/bash
# Temporarily disable function
aws lambda put-function-concurrency \
  --function-name anihpj-queue-processor \
  --reserved-concurrent-executions 0 \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Function disabled (0 concurrency)"
{ "ReservedConcurrentExecutions": 0 } ✓ Function disabled (0 concurrency)
# Disable function from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Function disabled via 0 concurrency") print(f"No invocations will execute")
Example 4: Reserve for API Handler
#!/bin/bash
# High concurrency for API
aws lambda put-function-concurrency \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --reserved-concurrent-executions 500 \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ 500 instances reserved for API"
{ "ReservedConcurrentExecutions": 500 } ✓ 500 instances reserved for API
# API handler capacity from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"API Handler: 500 reserved") print(f"✓ Ready for high traffic")
Example 5: Throttle Report Generator
#!/bin/bash
# Limit heavy reports to prevent resource exhaustion
aws lambda put-function-concurrency \
  --function-name anihpj-report-generator \
  --reserved-concurrent-executions 5 \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Reports limited to 5 concurrent"
{ "ReservedConcurrentExecutions": 5 } ✓ Reports limited to 5 concurrent
# Throttle reports from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Report generator: max 5 concurrent") print(f"✓ Heavy tasks throttled")

get-function-concurrency

Gets the reserved concurrency setting for a Lambda function. Check if capacity is reserved or if the function uses unreserved account concurrency.

Syntax
aws lambda get-function-concurrency \
  --function-name  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name
Return Value
  • ReservedConcurrentExecutions (Integer): Reserved capacity (null if unreserved)
Example 1: Check Reserved Concurrency
#!/bin/bash
# Get concurrency setting
aws lambda get-function-concurrency \
  --function-name anihpj-email-sender \
  --region us-east-1
{ "ReservedConcurrentExecutions": 100 }
# Check concurrency from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost reserved = 100 print(f"Reserved: {reserved} instances")
Example 2: Check Unreserved Function
#!/bin/bash
# Function without reserved concurrency
aws lambda get-function-concurrency \
  --function-name anihpj-image-processor \
  --region us-east-1
{}
# Unreserved function from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"No reserved concurrency") print(f"Uses shared account pool")
Example 3: Check Disabled Function
#!/bin/bash
# Function with 0 concurrency (disabled)
aws lambda get-function-concurrency \
  --function-name anihpj-deprecated-handler \
  --region us-east-1
{ "ReservedConcurrentExecutions": 0 }
# Disabled function from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Concurrency: 0") print(f"Function is DISABLED")
Example 4: Audit All Functions
#!/bin/bash
# Check concurrency for all anihpj functions
for FUNC in $(aws lambda list-functions \
  --query 'Functions[?contains(FunctionName,`anihpj`)].FunctionName' \
  --output text); do
  CONCURRENCY=$(aws lambda get-function-concurrency \
    --function-name $FUNC \
    --query 'ReservedConcurrentExecutions' \
    --output text)
  echo "$FUNC: ${CONCURRENCY:-unreserved}"
done
anihpj-email-sender: 100 anihpj-api-handler: 500 anihpj-db-sync: 10 anihpj-image-processor: unreserved
# Audit concurrency from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost audit = {'reserved': 3, 'unreserved': 1} print(f"Concurrency audit complete")
Example 5: Calculate Used Concurrency
#!/bin/bash
# Sum reserved concurrency
TOTAL=$(aws lambda list-functions --query 'Functions[*].FunctionName' --output text | \
  xargs -I {} aws lambda get-function-concurrency \
    --function-name {} \
    --query 'ReservedConcurrentExecutions' \
    --output text 2>/dev/null | \
  grep -v None | awk '{sum+=$1} END {print sum}')

echo "Total reserved concurrency: $TOTAL"
Total reserved concurrency: 610
# Calculate total from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost total_reserved = 610 account_limit = 1000 print(f"Reserved: {total_reserved}/{account_limit}")

delete-function-concurrency

Removes reserved concurrency from a Lambda function. Function returns to using unreserved account concurrency pool. Use to re-enable a disabled function (set to 0) or release reserved capacity.

Syntax
aws lambda delete-function-concurrency \
  --function-name  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name
Return Value
  • No response body on success (HTTP 204)
Example 1: Remove Reserved Concurrency
#!/bin/bash
# Remove reservation, use account pool
aws lambda delete-function-concurrency \
  --function-name anihpj-email-sender \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Concurrency reservation removed"
✓ Concurrency reservation removed
# Remove reservation from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Now using unreserved pool")
Example 2: Re-enable Disabled Function
#!/bin/bash
# Re-enable function (was set to 0)
aws lambda delete-function-concurrency \
  --function-name anihpj-queue-processor \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Function re-enabled"
✓ Function re-enabled
# Re-enable function from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Function was disabled (0)") print(f"Now enabled with shared pool")
Example 3: Release Capacity
#!/bin/bash
# Release capacity for other functions
FUNCTION="anihpj-old-api-handler"

CURRENT=$(aws lambda get-function-concurrency \
  --function-name $FUNCTION \
  --query 'ReservedConcurrentExecutions' \
  --output text)

aws lambda delete-function-concurrency \
  --function-name $FUNCTION

echo "✓ Released $CURRENT reserved instances"
✓ Released 200 reserved instances
# Release capacity from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost released = 200 print(f"Released {released} for other functions")
Example 4: Cleanup All Reservations
#!/bin/bash
# Remove all concurrency reservations
for FUNC in $(aws lambda list-functions \
  --query 'Functions[*].FunctionName' \
  --output text); do
  aws lambda delete-function-concurrency --function-name $FUNC 2>/dev/null
done

echo "✓ All reservations cleared"
✓ All reservations cleared
# Cleanup reservations from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All functions now use shared pool")
Example 5: Safe Removal with Check
#!/bin/bash
# Check before removing
FUNCTION="anihpj-report-generator"

RESERVED=$(aws lambda get-function-concurrency \
  --function-name $FUNCTION \
  --query 'ReservedConcurrentExecutions' \
  --output text)

if [ "$RESERVED" != "None" ]; then
  read -p "Remove $RESERVED reserved from $FUNCTION? (y/n): " confirm
  if [ "$confirm" = "y" ]; then
    aws lambda delete-function-concurrency --function-name $FUNCTION
    echo "✓ Removed"
  fi
else
  echo "No reservation exists"
fi
Remove 5 reserved from anihpj-report-generator? (y/n): y ✓ Removed
# Safe removal from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"User confirmed removal")

create-function-url-config

Creates a function URL for direct HTTPS invocation without API Gateway. Simpler alternative for webhooks, simple APIs, or Django background task triggers with a dedicated URL endpoint.

Syntax
aws lambda create-function-url-config \
  --function-name  \
  --auth-type  \
  [--cors ] \
  [--qualifier ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name
  • --auth-type (string): [Required] NONE (public) or AWS_IAM
  • --cors (structure): CORS configuration
  • --qualifier (string): Alias for the URL
Return Value
  • FunctionUrl (String): The generated HTTPS URL
  • AuthType (String): Authentication type
  • Cors (Object): CORS settings
Example 1: Create Public URL
#!/bin/bash
# Create public webhook endpoint
aws lambda create-function-url-config \
  --function-name anihpj-webhook-handler \
  --auth-type NONE \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Public URL created"
{ "FunctionUrl": "https://abc123xyz.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-webhook-handler", "AuthType": "NONE", "CreationTime": "2026-01-13T15:00:00.000Z" } ✓ Public URL created
# Create public URL from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost url = "https://abc123xyz.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/" print(f"Webhook URL: {url}")
Example 2: Create with CORS
#!/bin/bash
# Enable CORS for browser access
aws lambda create-function-url-config \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --auth-type NONE \
  --cors '{
    "AllowOrigins": ["https://anihpj.com", "https://www.anihpj.com"],
    "AllowMethods": ["GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE"],
    "AllowHeaders": ["Content-Type", "Authorization"],
    "MaxAge": 86400
  }' \
  --region us-east-1
{ "FunctionUrl": "https://def456uvw.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/", "AuthType": "NONE", "Cors": { "AllowOrigins": ["https://anihpj.com", "https://www.anihpj.com"], "AllowMethods": ["GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE"], "AllowHeaders": ["Content-Type", "Authorization"], "MaxAge": 86400 } }
# URL with CORS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"CORS enabled for anihpj.com") print(f"✓ Browser access allowed")
Example 3: Create IAM-Protected URL
#!/bin/bash
# Require IAM authentication
aws lambda create-function-url-config \
  --function-name anihpj-internal-api \
  --auth-type AWS_IAM \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ IAM-protected URL created"
{ "FunctionUrl": "https://ghi789rst.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/", "AuthType": "AWS_IAM" } ✓ IAM-protected URL created
# IAM-protected URL from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"URL requires IAM credentials") print(f"✓ Secure internal endpoint")
Example 4: Create URL for Alias
#!/bin/bash
# Create URL for prod alias
aws lambda create-function-url-config \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --qualifier prod \
  --auth-type NONE \
  --cors '{"AllowOrigins": ["*"]}' \
  --region us-east-1
{ "FunctionUrl": "https://jkl012mno.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-api-handler:prod", "AuthType": "NONE" }
# URL for alias from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Prod alias has dedicated URL") print(f"Stable endpoint for production")
Example 5: Create Wildcard CORS URL
#!/bin/bash
# Allow all origins (development)
aws lambda create-function-url-config \
  --function-name anihpj-dev-api \
  --auth-type NONE \
  --cors '{
    "AllowOrigins": ["*"],
    "AllowMethods": ["*"],
    "AllowHeaders": ["*"],
    "ExposeHeaders": ["X-Request-Id"],
    "AllowCredentials": false
  }' \
  --region us-east-1
{ "FunctionUrl": "https://pqr345stu.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/", "Cors": { "AllowOrigins": ["*"], "AllowMethods": ["*"], "AllowHeaders": ["*"] } }
# Wildcard CORS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All origins allowed (dev only)") print(f"⚠️ Restrict in production")

get-function-url-config

Gets the function URL configuration including the URL endpoint, authentication type, and CORS settings. Retrieve the URL to share with external services or verify configuration.

Syntax
aws lambda get-function-url-config \
  --function-name  \
  [--qualifier ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name
  • --qualifier (string): Alias name
Return Value
  • FunctionUrl (String): The HTTPS URL
  • AuthType (String): NONE or AWS_IAM
  • Cors (Object): CORS configuration
Example 1: Get Function URL
#!/bin/bash
# Get the function URL
aws lambda get-function-url-config \
  --function-name anihpj-webhook-handler \
  --region us-east-1
{ "FunctionUrl": "https://abc123xyz.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-webhook-handler", "AuthType": "NONE", "Cors": {}, "CreationTime": "2026-01-13T15:00:00.000Z", "LastModifiedTime": "2026-01-13T15:00:00.000Z" }
# Get URL config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost url = "https://abc123xyz.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/" print(f"URL: {url}")
Example 2: Extract URL Only
#!/bin/bash
# Get just the URL
FUNCTION_URL=$(aws lambda get-function-url-config \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --query 'FunctionUrl' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1)

echo "Endpoint: $FUNCTION_URL"
Endpoint: https://def456uvw.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/
# Extract URL from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Webhook endpoint extracted")
Example 3: Check CORS Settings
#!/bin/bash
# Verify CORS configuration
aws lambda get-function-url-config \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --query 'Cors' \
  --region us-east-1
{ "AllowOrigins": ["https://anihpj.com"], "AllowMethods": ["GET", "POST"], "AllowHeaders": ["Content-Type"], "MaxAge": 86400 }
# Check CORS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost cors = {'origins': ['https://anihpj.com']} print(f"CORS: {cors}")
Example 4: Get Alias URL
#!/bin/bash
# Get URL for production alias
aws lambda get-function-url-config \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --qualifier prod \
  --query '{URL:FunctionUrl,Auth:AuthType}' \
  --region us-east-1
{ "URL": "https://jkl012mno.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/", "Auth": "NONE" }
# Alias URL from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Prod alias URL retrieved")
Example 5: Export All URLs
#!/bin/bash
# Get URLs for all functions
for FUNC in $(aws lambda list-functions \
  --query 'Functions[?contains(FunctionName,`anihpj`)].FunctionName' \
  --output text); do
  URL=$(aws lambda get-function-url-config \
    --function-name $FUNC \
    --query 'FunctionUrl' \
    --output text 2>/dev/null)
  if [ "$URL" != "None" ]; then
    echo "$FUNC: $URL"
  fi
done
anihpj-webhook-handler: https://abc123xyz.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/ anihpj-api-handler: https://def456uvw.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/
# Export all URLs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost urls_found = 2 print(f"Found {urls_found} function URLs")

delete-function-url-config

Deletes a function URL configuration. Removes the HTTPS endpoint and disables direct invocation via URL. Use when migrating to API Gateway or retiring public endpoints.

Syntax
aws lambda delete-function-url-config \
  --function-name  \
  [--qualifier ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name
  • --qualifier (string): Alias name
Return Value
  • No response body on success (HTTP 204)
Example 1: Delete Function URL
#!/bin/bash
# Remove function URL
aws lambda delete-function-url-config \
  --function-name anihpj-webhook-handler \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Function URL deleted"
✓ Function URL deleted
# Delete URL from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Function URL removed")
Example 2: Delete Alias URL
#!/bin/bash
# Remove URL for specific alias
aws lambda delete-function-url-config \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --qualifier dev \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Dev alias URL removed"
✓ Dev alias URL removed
# Delete alias URL from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Dev URL removed, prod remains")
Example 3: Migrate to API Gateway
#!/bin/bash
# Remove URL before API Gateway integration
FUNCTION="anihpj-api-handler"

echo "Migrating to API Gateway..."
# Delete function URL
aws lambda delete-function-url-config \
  --function-name $FUNCTION

# Add API Gateway permission
aws lambda add-permission \
  --function-name $FUNCTION \
  --statement-id APIGateway \
  --action lambda:InvokeFunction \
  --principal apigateway.amazonaws.com

echo "✓ Migrated to API Gateway"
Migrating to API Gateway... ✓ Migrated to API Gateway
# Migrate to API Gateway from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Function URL → API Gateway") print(f"✓ Migration complete")
Example 4: Cleanup All URLs
#!/bin/bash
# Remove all function URLs
for FUNC in $(aws lambda list-functions \
  --query 'Functions[*].FunctionName' \
  --output text); do
  aws lambda delete-function-url-config \
    --function-name $FUNC 2>/dev/null && \
    echo "Deleted URL: $FUNC"
done
Deleted URL: anihpj-webhook-handler Deleted URL: anihpj-api-handler
# Cleanup URLs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost deleted = 2 print(f"Removed {deleted} function URLs")
Example 5: Safe Delete with Verification
#!/bin/bash
# Verify and delete
FUNCTION="anihpj-old-endpoint"

URL=$(aws lambda get-function-url-config \
  --function-name $FUNCTION \
  --query 'FunctionUrl' \
  --output text 2>/dev/null)

if [ -n "$URL" ] && [ "$URL" != "None" ]; then
  read -p "Delete $URL? (y/n): " confirm
  if [ "$confirm" = "y" ]; then
    aws lambda delete-function-url-config --function-name $FUNCTION
    echo "✓ Deleted"
  fi
else
  echo "No URL configured"
fi
Delete https://xyz789.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/? (y/n): y ✓ Deleted
# Safe delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"User verified before deletion")

publish-layer-version

Creates a new version of a Lambda layer with shared code or dependencies. Package Django libraries, database drivers, or common utilities once and share across multiple functions.

Syntax
aws lambda publish-layer-version \
  --layer-name  \
  [--description ] \
  [--content S3Bucket=,S3Key= | --zip-file fileb://] \
  [--compatible-runtimes ] \
  [--license-info ] \
  [--compatible-architectures ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --layer-name (string): [Required] Layer name
  • --content (structure): Layer content (S3 or ZIP)
  • --compatible-runtimes (list): Compatible runtimes
  • --description (string): Version description
  • --compatible-architectures (list): x86_64, arm64
Return Value
  • LayerArn (String): Layer ARN
  • LayerVersionArn (String): Version-specific ARN
  • Version (Integer): Version number
Example 1: Create Django Dependencies Layer
#!/bin/bash
# Publish Django dependencies layer
aws lambda publish-layer-version \
  --layer-name django-deps \
  --description "Django 5.0, psycopg2, boto3" \
  --zip-file fileb://django_layer.zip \
  --compatible-runtimes python3.12 python3.11 \
  --compatible-architectures x86_64 \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Django layer published"
{ "LayerArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:django-deps", "LayerVersionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:django-deps:6", "Version": 6, "Description": "Django 5.0, psycopg2, boto3", "CompatibleRuntimes": ["python3.12", "python3.11"] } ✓ Django layer published
# Publish Django layer from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost layer = {'name': 'django-deps', 'version': 6} print(f"Layer: {layer['name']}:{layer['version']}")
Example 2: Publish from S3
#!/bin/bash
# Publish layer from S3
aws lambda publish-layer-version \
  --layer-name pandas-numpy \
  --description "Pandas 2.0 + NumPy for data processing" \
  --content S3Bucket=anihpj-layers,S3Key=pandas-numpy-v2.zip \
  --compatible-runtimes python3.12 \
  --region us-east-1
{ "LayerArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:pandas-numpy", "Version": 4, "Description": "Pandas 2.0 + NumPy for data processing" }
# Layer from S3 from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"pandas-numpy:4 published from S3")
Example 3: Create PIL/Pillow Layer
#!/bin/bash
# Image processing layer
aws lambda publish-layer-version \
  --layer-name pillow-imaging \
  --description "Pillow for image resizing" \
  --zip-file fileb://pillow_layer.zip \
  --compatible-runtimes python3.12 \
  --compatible-architectures x86_64 arm64 \
  --region us-east-1
{ "LayerArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:pillow-imaging", "Version": 2, "CompatibleArchitectures": ["x86_64", "arm64"] }
# Pillow layer from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Pillow layer supports ARM + x86")
Example 4: Shared Utilities Layer
#!/bin/bash
# Common utilities for all anihpj functions
aws lambda publish-layer-version \
  --layer-name anihpj-utils \
  --description "Shared logging, error handling, DB helpers" \
  --zip-file fileb://utils_layer.zip \
  --compatible-runtimes python3.12 \
  --license-info "MIT" \
  --region us-east-1
{ "LayerVersionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:anihpj-utils:3", "Version": 3, "LicenseInfo": "MIT" }
# Utilities layer from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Shared utilities: logging, DB helpers")
Example 5: Get Layer ARN
#!/bin/bash
# Publish and capture ARN for use
LAYER_ARN=$(aws lambda publish-layer-version \
  --layer-name crypto-libs \
  --description "Cryptography libraries" \
  --zip-file fileb://crypto.zip \
  --compatible-runtimes python3.12 \
  --query 'LayerVersionArn' \
  --output text)

echo "Use this ARN: $LAYER_ARN"
Use this ARN: arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:crypto-libs:1
# Get layer ARN from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost arn = "arn:aws:lambda:...:layer:crypto-libs:1" print(f"Layer ARN for function config")

list-layers

Lists all Lambda layers in your account. View available shared dependency layers for Django functions, filter by runtime compatibility.

Syntax
aws lambda list-layers \
  [--compatible-runtime ] \
  [--compatible-architecture ] \
  [--max-items ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --compatible-runtime (string): Filter by runtime
  • --compatible-architecture (string): x86_64 or arm64
  • --max-items (integer): Maximum layers to return
Return Value
  • Layers (List): Array of layer summaries
  • NextMarker (String): Pagination token
Example 1: List All Layers
#!/bin/bash
# List all Lambda layers
aws lambda list-layers \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Layers": [ { "LayerName": "django-deps", "LayerArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:django-deps", "LatestMatchingVersion": { "Version": 6, "Description": "Django 5.0, psycopg2, boto3" } }, { "LayerName": "pandas-numpy", "LayerArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:pandas-numpy", "LatestMatchingVersion": { "Version": 4 } } ] }
# List layers from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost layers = ['django-deps', 'pandas-numpy'] print(f"Found {len(layers)} layers")
Example 2: Filter by Runtime
#!/bin/bash
# Python 3.12 compatible layers
aws lambda list-layers \
  --compatible-runtime python3.12 \
  --query 'Layers[*].LayerName' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1
------------------------ | ListLayers | +----------------------+ | django-deps | | pandas-numpy | | pillow-imaging | | anihpj-utils | +----------------------+
# Python 3.12 layers from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost python312_layers = 4 print(f"{python312_layers} layers for Python 3.12")
Example 3: Filter by Architecture
#!/bin/bash
# ARM64 compatible layers
aws lambda list-layers \
  --compatible-architecture arm64 \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Layers": [ {"LayerName": "pillow-imaging", "LatestMatchingVersion": {"Version": 2}} ] }
# ARM64 layers from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost arm_layers = 1 print(f"{arm_layers} layers support ARM64")
Example 4: Get Layer Details
#!/bin/bash
# Layer names with versions and descriptions
aws lambda list-layers \
  --query 'Layers[*].{Name:LayerName,Version:LatestMatchingVersion.Version,Desc:LatestMatchingVersion.Description}' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1
---------------------------------------------------- | ListLayers | +----------------+---------+-----------------------+ | Name | Version | Desc | +----------------+---------+-----------------------+ | django-deps | 6 | Django 5.0, psycopg2 | | pandas-numpy | 4 | Pandas 2.0 + NumPy | | pillow-imaging | 2 | Pillow for resizing | +----------------+---------+-----------------------+
# Layer details from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Layer inventory with versions")
Example 5: Export Layer ARNs
#!/bin/bash
# Export latest layer ARNs for use
aws lambda list-layers \
  --compatible-runtime python3.12 \
  --query 'Layers[*].LatestMatchingVersion.LayerVersionArn' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1
arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:django-deps:6 arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:pandas-numpy:4 arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:pillow-imaging:2
# Export ARNs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Layer ARNs for function config")

list-layer-versions

Lists all versions of a Lambda layer. View version history, find stable versions for production, or identify old versions for cleanup.

Syntax
aws lambda list-layer-versions \
  --layer-name  \
  [--compatible-runtime ] \
  [--compatible-architecture ] \
  [--max-items ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --layer-name (string): [Required] Layer name
  • --compatible-runtime (string): Filter by runtime
  • --compatible-architecture (string): x86_64 or arm64
Return Value
  • LayerVersions (List): Array of layer versions
Example 1: List All Versions
#!/bin/bash
# List all versions of django-deps layer
aws lambda list-layer-versions \
  --layer-name django-deps \
  --region us-east-1
{ "LayerVersions": [ {"Version": 6, "Description": "Django 5.0, psycopg2, boto3"}, {"Version": 5, "Description": "Django 4.2, psycopg2"}, {"Version": 4, "Description": "Django 4.1"}, {"Version": 3, "Description": "Django 4.0"}, {"Version": 2, "Description": "Django 3.2"}, {"Version": 1, "Description": "Initial"} ] }
# List versions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost versions = [6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1] print(f"django-deps has {len(versions)} versions")
Example 2: Table Format
#!/bin/bash
# Display as table
aws lambda list-layer-versions \
  --layer-name django-deps \
  --query 'LayerVersions[*].{Version:Version,Description:Description,Created:CreatedDate}' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1
--------------------------------------------------------- | ListLayerVersions | +---------+---------------------------+-----------------+ | Version | Description | Created | +---------+---------------------------+-----------------+ | 6 | Django 5.0, psycopg2 | 2026-01-10 | | 5 | Django 4.2, psycopg2 | 2025-06-15 | | 4 | Django 4.1 | 2025-01-20 | +---------+---------------------------+-----------------+
# Version table from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version history with dates")
Example 3: Get Latest Version ARN
#!/bin/bash
# Get ARN of latest version
LATEST_ARN=$(aws lambda list-layer-versions \
  --layer-name pandas-numpy \
  --query 'LayerVersions[0].LayerVersionArn' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1)

echo "Latest: $LATEST_ARN"
Latest: arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:pandas-numpy:4
# Latest version ARN from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Latest ARN for function update")
Example 4: Filter by Runtime
#!/bin/bash
# Python 3.12 compatible versions only
aws lambda list-layer-versions \
  --layer-name pillow-imaging \
  --compatible-runtime python3.12 \
  --query 'LayerVersions[*].Version' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1
2 1
# Filter by runtime from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost compatible = [2, 1] print(f"Python 3.12 compatible: {compatible}")
Example 5: Count Versions for Cleanup
#!/bin/bash
# Count versions and identify old ones
LAYER="django-deps"

VERSION_COUNT=$(aws lambda list-layer-versions \
  --layer-name $LAYER \
  --query 'length(LayerVersions)' \
  --output text)

echo "Total versions: $VERSION_COUNT"

if [ $VERSION_COUNT -gt 5 ]; then
  echo "Consider cleaning up old versions"
fi
Total versions: 6 Consider cleaning up old versions
# Count versions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost count = 6 print(f"{count} versions - cleanup recommended")

get-layer-version

Gets information about a specific layer version including download URL for the layer content. Retrieve layer details for debugging or backup.

Syntax
aws lambda get-layer-version \
  --layer-name  \
  --version-number  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --layer-name (string): [Required] Layer name
  • --version-number (integer): [Required] Version number
Return Value
  • Content (Object): Location with download URL
  • LayerVersionArn (String): Version ARN
  • Version (Integer): Version number
  • CompatibleRuntimes (List): Supported runtimes
Example 1: Get Layer Version Details
#!/bin/bash
# Get layer version info
aws lambda get-layer-version \
  --layer-name django-deps \
  --version-number 6 \
  --region us-east-1
{ "LayerVersionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:django-deps:6", "Version": 6, "Description": "Django 5.0, psycopg2, boto3", "CreatedDate": "2026-01-10T10:00:00.000+0000", "CompatibleRuntimes": ["python3.12", "python3.11"], "Content": { "Location": "https://awslambda-us-east-1-layers.s3...", "CodeSize": 52428800 } }
# Get layer details from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost layer = {'name': 'django-deps', 'version': 6, 'size': '50MB'} print(f"Layer: {layer}")
Example 2: Download Layer Content
#!/bin/bash
# Download layer ZIP for inspection
LAYER_URL=$(aws lambda get-layer-version \
  --layer-name django-deps \
  --version-number 6 \
  --query 'Content.Location' \
  --output text)

curl -o django_layer.zip "$LAYER_URL"
echo "✓ Layer downloaded"
% Total % Received 100 50M 100 50M ✓ Layer downloaded
# Download layer from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Layer content downloaded for inspection")
Example 3: Get ARN for Function
#!/bin/bash
# Get version ARN to use in function
ARN=$(aws lambda get-layer-version \
  --layer-name pandas-numpy \
  --version-number 4 \
  --query 'LayerVersionArn' \
  --output text)

echo "Use this ARN: $ARN"
Use this ARN: arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:pandas-numpy:4
# Get ARN from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost arn = "arn:aws:lambda:...:layer:pandas-numpy:4" print(f"ARN for function layers config")
Example 4: Check Compatible Runtimes
#!/bin/bash
# Verify runtime compatibility
aws lambda get-layer-version \
  --layer-name pillow-imaging \
  --version-number 2 \
  --query 'CompatibleRuntimes' \
  --region us-east-1
[ "python3.12", "python3.11" ]
# Check runtimes from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost runtimes = ['python3.12', 'python3.11'] print(f"Compatible: {runtimes}")
Example 5: Get Layer Size
#!/bin/bash
# Check layer size
SIZE=$(aws lambda get-layer-version \
  --layer-name django-deps \
  --version-number 6 \
  --query 'Content.CodeSize' \
  --output text)

SIZE_MB=$((SIZE / 1024 / 1024))
echo "Layer size: ${SIZE_MB}MB"
Layer size: 50MB
# Layer size from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost size_mb = 50 print(f"Layer size: {size_mb}MB")

delete-layer-version

Deletes a specific version of a Lambda layer. Use for cleanup of old layer versions. Cannot delete a layer version in use by functions.

Syntax
aws lambda delete-layer-version \
  --layer-name  \
  --version-number  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --layer-name (string): [Required] Layer name
  • --version-number (integer): [Required] Version to delete
Return Value
  • None: No output on successful deletion
Example 1: Delete Old Layer Version
#!/bin/bash
# Delete old layer version
aws lambda delete-layer-version \
  --layer-name django-deps \
  --version-number 3 \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Layer version 3 deleted"
✓ Layer version 3 deleted
# Delete layer version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Old layer version removed")
Example 2: Clean Up All Old Versions
#!/bin/bash
# Delete all versions except latest
LAYER="django-deps"
LATEST=$(aws lambda list-layer-versions \
  --layer-name $LAYER \
  --query 'LayerVersions[0].Version' \
  --output text)

for VERSION in $(aws lambda list-layer-versions \
  --layer-name $LAYER \
  --query "LayerVersions[?Version!=\`$LATEST\`].Version" \
  --output text); do
    aws lambda delete-layer-version \
      --layer-name $LAYER \
      --version-number $VERSION
    echo "Deleted version $VERSION"
done

echo "✓ Cleanup complete"
Deleted version 5 Deleted version 4 Deleted version 3 ✓ Cleanup complete
# Cleanup old versions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost deleted = [5, 4, 3] print(f"Deleted versions: {deleted}")
Example 3: Delete Unused Layer
#!/bin/bash
# Delete test layer version
aws lambda delete-layer-version \
  --layer-name test-utils \
  --version-number 1 \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Test layer removed"
✓ Test layer removed
# Remove test layer from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Test layer cleaned up")
Example 4: Safe Delete with Check
#!/bin/bash
# Check before deleting
LAYER="pandas-numpy"
VERSION=2

# List functions using this layer
FUNCTIONS=$(aws lambda list-functions \
  --query "Functions[?contains(Layers[].Arn, ':layer:$LAYER:$VERSION')]" \
  --output text)

if [ -z "$FUNCTIONS" ]; then
    aws lambda delete-layer-version \
      --layer-name $LAYER \
      --version-number $VERSION
    echo "✓ Deleted safely"
else
    echo "Layer in use!"
fi
✓ Deleted safely
# Safe delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Layer deleted after usage check")
Example 5: Bulk Delete by Age
#!/bin/bash
# Delete versions older than cutoff
LAYER="django-deps"
CUTOFF="2025-01-01"

aws lambda list-layer-versions \
  --layer-name $LAYER \
  --query "LayerVersions[?CreatedDate<'$CUTOFF'].Version" \
  --output text | while read VERSION; do
    aws lambda delete-layer-version \
      --layer-name $LAYER \
      --version-number $VERSION
    echo "Deleted old version $VERSION"
done
Deleted old version 2 Deleted old version 1
# Delete old versions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost old = [2, 1] print(f"Old versions removed: {old}")

tag-resource

Adds tags to a Lambda function for cost allocation, organization, and filtering. Tags are key-value pairs for resource management.

Syntax
aws lambda tag-resource \
  --resource  \
  --tags  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --resource (string): [Required] Function ARN (not just name)
  • --tags (map): [Required] Key=Value pairs
Return Value
  • None: No output on success
Example 1: Tag for Cost Allocation
#!/bin/bash
# Tag function for cost tracking
ARN="arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-job-processor"

aws lambda tag-resource \
  --resource $ARN \
  --tags Project=anihpj,Environment=production,CostCenter=Engineering

echo "✓ Tags added"
✓ Tags added
# Tag for costs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost tags = {'Project': 'anihpj', 'Environment': 'production'} print(f"Cost allocation tags: {tags}")
Example 2: Tag Multiple Functions
#!/bin/bash
# Tag all jobpost functions
ACCOUNT="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"

for FUNC in anihpj-job-processor anihpj-notify anihpj-cleanup; do
    ARN="arn:aws:lambda:$REGION:$ACCOUNT:function:$FUNC"
    aws lambda tag-resource \
      --resource $ARN \
      --tags App=jobpost,Team=backend
    echo "Tagged: $FUNC"
done
Tagged: anihpj-job-processor Tagged: anihpj-notify Tagged: anihpj-cleanup
# Tag functions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost funcs = ['job-processor', 'notify', 'cleanup'] print(f"Tagged {len(funcs)} functions")
Example 3: Environment Tags
#!/bin/bash
# Tag with environment info
aws lambda tag-resource \
  --resource arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-api \
  --tags Environment=staging,Version=2.1.0,DeployedBy=CICD

echo "✓ Environment tagged"
✓ Environment tagged
# Environment tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost tags = {'Environment': 'staging', 'Version': '2.1.0'} print(f"Tags: {tags}")
Example 4: Compliance Tags
#!/bin/bash
# Add compliance tracking tags
aws lambda tag-resource \
  --resource arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-job-processor \
  --tags DataClassification=Confidential,Compliance=GDPR,Owner=devops@anihpj.com

echo "✓ Compliance tags added"
✓ Compliance tags added
# Compliance tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost tags = {'DataClassification': 'Confidential', 'Compliance': 'GDPR'} print(f"Compliance: {tags}")
Example 5: Update Existing Tags
#!/bin/bash
# Update tags (overwrites existing keys)
aws lambda tag-resource \
  --resource arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-job-processor \
  --tags Environment=production,LastUpdated=2026-01-15

echo "✓ Tags updated"
✓ Tags updated
# Update tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment tag updated to production")

untag-resource

Removes tags from a Lambda function. Specify tag keys to remove. Use for cleaning up obsolete tags or correcting tagging mistakes.

Syntax
aws lambda untag-resource \
  --resource  \
  --tag-keys  [...] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --resource (string): [Required] Function ARN
  • --tag-keys (list): [Required] Tag keys to remove
Return Value
  • None: No output on success
Example 1: Remove Single Tag
#!/bin/bash
# Remove a tag from function
aws lambda untag-resource \
  --resource arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-job-processor \
  --tag-keys Environment

echo "✓ Environment tag removed"
✓ Environment tag removed
# Remove tag from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment tag removed")
Example 2: Remove Multiple Tags
#!/bin/bash
# Remove multiple tags
aws lambda untag-resource \
  --resource arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-api \
  --tag-keys TempTag DebugMode TestFlag

echo "✓ Multiple tags removed"
✓ Multiple tags removed
# Remove multiple tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost removed = ['TempTag', 'DebugMode', 'TestFlag'] print(f"Removed: {removed}")
Example 3: Clean Up All Functions
#!/bin/bash
# Remove obsolete tag from all functions
TAG_KEY="OldProject"
ACCOUNT="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"

for FUNC in $(aws lambda list-functions \
  --query 'Functions[].FunctionName' \
  --output text); do
    ARN="arn:aws:lambda:$REGION:$ACCOUNT:function:$FUNC"
    aws lambda untag-resource \
      --resource $ARN \
      --tag-keys $TAG_KEY 2>/dev/null
done

echo "✓ Cleanup complete"
✓ Cleanup complete
# Cleanup tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"OldProject tag removed from all")
Example 4: Remove Development Tags
#!/bin/bash
# Remove dev-only tags before production
aws lambda untag-resource \
  --resource arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-job-processor \
  --tag-keys Developer TicketNumber SprintID

echo "✓ Dev tags removed for prod"
✓ Dev tags removed for prod
# Pre-production cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost dev_tags = ['Developer', 'TicketNumber'] print(f"Dev tags removed: {dev_tags}")
Example 5: Conditional Untag
#!/bin/bash
# Remove tag only if it exists
ARN="arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-api"
TAG_KEY="Deprecated"

if aws lambda list-tags --resource $ARN \
  --query "Tags.$TAG_KEY" --output text | grep -q .; then
    aws lambda untag-resource \
      --resource $ARN \
      --tag-keys $TAG_KEY
    echo "✓ Removed: $TAG_KEY"
else
    echo "Tag not found"
fi
✓ Removed: Deprecated
# Conditional untag from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Tag removed after existence check")

list-tags

Lists all tags associated with a Lambda function. Returns key-value pairs for cost tracking, organization, and resource filtering.

Syntax
aws lambda list-tags \
  --resource  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --resource (string): [Required] Function ARN
Return Value
  • Tags (Object): Map of tag key-value pairs
Example 1: List All Tags
#!/bin/bash
# List function tags
aws lambda list-tags \
  --resource arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-job-processor \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Tags": { "Project": "anihpj", "Environment": "production", "Team": "backend", "CostCenter": "Engineering" } }
# List tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost tags = {'Project': 'anihpj', 'Environment': 'production'} print(f"Function tags: {tags}")
Example 2: Get Specific Tag Value
#!/bin/bash
# Get environment tag
ENV=$(aws lambda list-tags \
  --resource arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-api \
  --query 'Tags.Environment' \
  --output text)

echo "Environment: $ENV"
Environment: production
# Get specific tag from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost env = 'production' print(f"Environment: {env}")
Example 3: Audit All Function Tags
#!/bin/bash
# List tags for all functions
ACCOUNT="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"

for FUNC in $(aws lambda list-functions \
  --query 'Functions[].FunctionName' \
  --output text); do
    ARN="arn:aws:lambda:$REGION:$ACCOUNT:function:$FUNC"
    echo "=== $FUNC ==="
    aws lambda list-tags --resource $ARN \
      --query 'Tags' --output table
done
=== anihpj-job-processor === ----------------------------------- | Project | anihpj | | Environment | production | ----------------------------------- === anihpj-api === ----------------------------------- | Project | anihpj | | Environment | staging | -----------------------------------
# Audit all tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost functions = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"Audited tags for {functions} functions")
Example 4: Find Untagged Functions
#!/bin/bash
# Find functions without required tags
ACCOUNT="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"

for FUNC in $(aws lambda list-functions \
  --query 'Functions[].FunctionName' \
  --output text); do
    ARN="arn:aws:lambda:$REGION:$ACCOUNT:function:$FUNC"
    
    PROJECT=$(aws lambda list-tags --resource $ARN \
      --query 'Tags.Project' --output text)
    
    if [ "$PROJECT" == "None" ]; then
        echo "MISSING Project tag: $FUNC"
    fi
done
MISSING Project tag: test-function MISSING Project tag: old-lambda
# Find untagged from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost untagged = ['test-function', 'old-lambda'] print(f"Untagged: {untagged}")
Example 5: Export Tags to CSV
#!/bin/bash
# Export all function tags to CSV
echo "Function,Project,Environment,Team" > lambda_tags.csv

ACCOUNT="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"

for FUNC in $(aws lambda list-functions \
  --query 'Functions[].FunctionName' \
  --output text); do
    ARN="arn:aws:lambda:$REGION:$ACCOUNT:function:$FUNC"
    
    TAGS=$(aws lambda list-tags --resource $ARN)
    PROJECT=$(echo $TAGS | jq -r '.Tags.Project // "N/A"')
    ENV=$(echo $TAGS | jq -r '.Tags.Environment // "N/A"')
    TEAM=$(echo $TAGS | jq -r '.Tags.Team // "N/A"')
    
    echo "$FUNC,$PROJECT,$ENV,$TEAM" >> lambda_tags.csv
done

echo "✓ Exported to lambda_tags.csv"
✓ Exported to lambda_tags.csv
# Export tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Tags exported to CSV for reporting")

AWS Elastic Beanstalk

Platform-as-a-Service for deploying Django applications with auto-scaling

📋 Elastic Beanstalk Overview

AWS Elastic Beanstalk is a fully managed Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) that automatically handles deployment, capacity provisioning, load balancing, auto-scaling, and health monitoring for your anihpj Django application. Simply upload your code and Beanstalk deploys it with best-practice configurations for JobPost, Skills, User, Author, and Location data handling.

Core Elastic Beanstalk Operations for Django

create-application - Create new EB application
create-environment - Deploy environment
create-application-version - Upload new version
update-environment - Update configuration
describe-environments - Get environment status
describe-environment-health - Health monitoring
swap-environment-cnames - Blue/green deployment
terminate-environment - Remove environment

create-application

Creates a new Elastic Beanstalk application container. An application is a logical collection of environments, versions, and configurations for your Django project.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \
  --application-name  \
  [--description ] \
  [--resource-lifecycle-config ] \
  [--tags ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string): [Required] Application name (1-100 chars)
  • --description (string): Description of the application
  • --resource-lifecycle-config (structure): Version lifecycle settings
  • --tags (list): Key-value tags for the application
Return Value
  • ApplicationName (String): Name of the application
  • ApplicationArn (String): ARN of the application
  • DateCreated (Timestamp): Creation date
  • DateUpdated (Timestamp): Last update date
Example 1: Create Django Application
#!/bin/bash
# Create Elastic Beanstalk application for Django
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --description "Django job posting application with Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost models" \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Application": { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "ApplicationArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:application/anihpj-jobpost", "Description": "Django job posting application with Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost models", "DateCreated": "2026-01-13T10:00:00.000Z", "DateUpdated": "2026-01-13T10:00:00.000Z" } }
# Create EB application from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost app_name = "anihpj-jobpost" print(f"EB Application created: {app_name}")
Example 2: Create with Tags
#!/bin/bash
# Create application with cost allocation tags
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --description "Production Django app" \
  --tags Key=Project,Value=anihpj Key=Environment,Value=production Key=Team,Value=backend \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Application": { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "ApplicationArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:application/anihpj-jobpost", "DateCreated": "2026-01-13T10:00:00.000Z" } }
# Create with tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost tags = {'Project': 'anihpj', 'Team': 'backend'} print(f"Application tagged: {tags}")
Example 3: Create with Lifecycle Policy
#!/bin/bash
# Create with version lifecycle to auto-delete old versions
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --resource-lifecycle-config '{
    "ServiceRole": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/aws-elasticbeanstalk-service-role",
    "VersionLifecycleConfig": {
      "MaxCountRule": {
        "Enabled": true,
        "MaxCount": 10,
        "DeleteSourceFromS3": true
      }
    }
  }' \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Application": { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "ResourceLifecycleConfig": { "VersionLifecycleConfig": { "MaxCountRule": { "Enabled": true, "MaxCount": 10 } } } } }
# Lifecycle policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Keep only 10 versions, auto-delete old")
Example 4: Create Staging Application
#!/bin/bash
# Create separate staging application
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost-staging \
  --description "Staging environment for testing" \
  --tags Key=Environment,Value=staging \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Staging application created"
{ "Application": { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost-staging", "Description": "Staging environment for testing" } } ✓ Staging application created
# Staging app from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Staging application for testing")
Example 5: Create and Verify
#!/bin/bash
# Create and immediately verify
APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"

aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \
  --application-name $APP_NAME \
  --description "Django jobpost app" \
  --region us-east-1

# Verify creation
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
  --application-names $APP_NAME \
  --query 'Applications[0].ApplicationName' \
  --output text
anihpj-jobpost
# Create and verify from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost app = "anihpj-jobpost" print(f"Verified: {app}")

describe-applications

Returns descriptions of existing Elastic Beanstalk applications. Use to list all applications or get details about specific ones.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
  [--application-names ...] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --application-names (list): Specific application names to describe (optional)
Return Value
  • Applications (Array): List of application descriptions
  • ApplicationName (String): Name of application
  • Description (String): Application description
  • Versions (Array): List of version labels
Example 1: List All Applications
#!/bin/bash
# List all EB applications
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Applications": [ { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "Description": "Django job posting application", "DateCreated": "2026-01-13T10:00:00.000Z", "Versions": ["v1.0.0", "v1.1.0", "v2.0.0"] } ] }
# List applications from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost apps = ['anihpj-jobpost'] print(f"Applications: {apps}")
Example 2: Get Specific Application
#!/bin/bash
# Get details for specific app
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
  --application-names anihpj-jobpost \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Applications": [ { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "ApplicationArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:application/anihpj-jobpost", "Description": "Django job posting application" } ] }
# Specific app from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"App: anihpj-jobpost")
Example 3: Get Application Names Only
#!/bin/bash
# List just application names
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
  --query 'Applications[].ApplicationName' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1
--------------------- | ApplicationName | --------------------- | anihpj-jobpost | | anihpj-staging | ---------------------
# App names from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost names = ['anihpj-jobpost', 'anihpj-staging'] print(f"Names: {names}")
Example 4: Check Application Exists
#!/bin/bash
# Check if application exists
APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"

if aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
  --application-names $APP_NAME \
  --query 'Applications[0].ApplicationName' \
  --output text 2>/dev/null | grep -q "$APP_NAME"; then
    echo "✓ Application exists"
else
    echo "✗ Application not found"
fi
✓ Application exists
# Check exists from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost exists = True print(f"App exists: {exists}")
Example 5: Get Version Count
#!/bin/bash
# Count versions for application
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
  --application-names anihpj-jobpost \
  --query 'Applications[0].Versions | length(@)' \
  --output text
5
# Version count from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost versions = 5 print(f"Total versions: {versions}")

update-application

Updates the description and resource lifecycle settings for an existing application. Use to modify app metadata or version cleanup policies.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk update-application \
  --application-name  \
  [--description ] \
  [--resource-lifecycle-config ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string): [Required] Name of application
  • --description (string): New description
  • --resource-lifecycle-config (structure): Version lifecycle settings
Return Value
  • Application (Object): Updated application info
  • DateUpdated (Timestamp): Update timestamp
Example 1: Update Description
#!/bin/bash
# Update application description
aws elasticbeanstalk update-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --description "Production Django job posting app v2.0" \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Application": { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "Description": "Production Django job posting app v2.0", "DateUpdated": "2026-01-13T12:00:00.000Z" } }
# Update description from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Description updated to v2.0")
Example 2: Set Lifecycle Policy
#!/bin/bash
# Set max 20 versions, auto-delete old
aws elasticbeanstalk update-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --resource-lifecycle-config '{
    "ServiceRole": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/aws-elasticbeanstalk-service-role",
    "VersionLifecycleConfig": {
      "MaxCountRule": {
        "Enabled": true,
        "MaxCount": 20,
        "DeleteSourceFromS3": true
      }
    }
  }'
{ "Application": { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "ResourceLifecycleConfig": { "VersionLifecycleConfig": { "MaxCountRule": {"Enabled": true, "MaxCount": 20} } } } }
# Lifecycle policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Max 20 versions policy set")
Example 3: Age-Based Cleanup
#!/bin/bash
# Delete versions older than 180 days
aws elasticbeanstalk update-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --resource-lifecycle-config '{
    "ServiceRole": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/aws-elasticbeanstalk-service-role",
    "VersionLifecycleConfig": {
      "MaxAgeRule": {
        "Enabled": true,
        "MaxAgeInDays": 180,
        "DeleteSourceFromS3": true
      }
    }
  }'
{ "Application": { "ResourceLifecycleConfig": { "VersionLifecycleConfig": { "MaxAgeRule": {"Enabled": true, "MaxAgeInDays": 180} } } } }
# Age-based cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Delete versions older than 180 days")
Example 4: Disable Lifecycle
#!/bin/bash
# Disable automatic version cleanup
aws elasticbeanstalk update-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --resource-lifecycle-config '{
    "VersionLifecycleConfig": {
      "MaxCountRule": {"Enabled": false},
      "MaxAgeRule": {"Enabled": false}
    }
  }'

echo "✓ Lifecycle rules disabled"
✓ Lifecycle rules disabled
# Disable lifecycle from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Lifecycle rules disabled")
Example 5: Update Both Settings
#!/bin/bash
# Update description and lifecycle together
aws elasticbeanstalk update-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --description "Django jobpost - Updated $(date +%Y-%m-%d)" \
  --resource-lifecycle-config '{
    "ServiceRole": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/aws-elasticbeanstalk-service-role",
    "VersionLifecycleConfig": {
      "MaxCountRule": {"Enabled": true, "MaxCount": 15}
    }
  }'
{ "Application": { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "Description": "Django jobpost - Updated 2026-01-13" } }
# Update both from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Description and lifecycle updated")

delete-application
