Deletes the specified application along with all associated versions and configurations. Cannot delete if environments are running.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application \
  --application-name  \
  [--terminate-env-by-force] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string): [Required] Name of application to delete
  • --terminate-env-by-force (boolean): Force terminate running environments
Return Value
  • None: No output on success
Example 1: Delete Application
#!/bin/bash
# Delete application (no running environments)
aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost-old \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Application deleted"
✓ Application deleted
# Delete app from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Application deleted")
Example 2: Force Delete with Environments
#!/bin/bash
# Force delete including running environments
aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost-test \
  --terminate-env-by-force \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ App and environments terminated"
✓ App and environments terminated
# Force delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Forced termination complete")
Example 3: Safe Delete with Check
#!/bin/bash
# Check for environments before deleting
APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost-staging"

ENVS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
  --application-name $APP_NAME \
  --query 'Environments[?Status!=`Terminated`].EnvironmentName' \
  --output text)

if [ -z "$ENVS" ]; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application \
      --application-name $APP_NAME
    echo "✓ Deleted safely"
else
    echo "Active environments: $ENVS"
fi
✓ Deleted safely
# Safe delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Deleted after environment check")
Example 4: Delete Staging App
#!/bin/bash
# Delete staging after promotion to production
aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost-staging \
  --terminate-env-by-force

echo "✓ Staging app cleaned up"
✓ Staging app cleaned up
# Delete staging from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Staging cleaned up")
Example 5: Delete Multiple Apps
#!/bin/bash
# Delete multiple test applications
for APP in test-app-1 test-app-2 test-app-3; do
    aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application \
      --application-name $APP \
      --terminate-env-by-force 2>/dev/null
    echo "Deleted: $APP"
done

echo "✓ Cleanup complete"
Deleted: test-app-1 Deleted: test-app-2 Deleted: test-app-3 ✓ Cleanup complete
# Delete multiple from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost deleted = ['test-app-1', 'test-app-2', 'test-app-3'] print(f"Deleted: {deleted}")

create-application-version

Creates a new application version from source bundle in S3. Each version represents a deployable iteration of your Django code.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application-version \
  --application-name  \
  --version-label  \
  [--description ] \
  [--source-bundle S3Bucket=,S3Key=] \
  [--auto-create-application] \
  [--process] \
  [--tags ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string): [Required] Application name
  • --version-label (string): [Required] Unique version label
  • --source-bundle (structure): S3 location of source ZIP
  • --description (string): Version description
  • --auto-create-application (boolean): Create app if not exists
  • --process (boolean): Pre-process and validate
Return Value
  • ApplicationVersion (Object): Version details
  • VersionLabel (String): The version label
  • Status (String): Processing status
Example 1: Create Version from S3
#!/bin/bash
# Create version from S3 source bundle
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application-version \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --version-label v2.1.0 \
  --description "Added new JobPost filtering features" \
  --source-bundle S3Bucket=anihpj-deployments,S3Key=releases/v2.1.0.zip \
  --region us-east-1
{ "ApplicationVersion": { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "VersionLabel": "v2.1.0", "Description": "Added new JobPost filtering features", "SourceBundle": { "S3Bucket": "anihpj-deployments", "S3Key": "releases/v2.1.0.zip" }, "DateCreated": "2026-01-13T10:00:00.000Z", "Status": "Unprocessed" } }
# Create version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost version = "v2.1.0" print(f"Version created: {version}")
Example 2: Upload and Create Version
#!/bin/bash
# Package Django app and create version
VERSION="v$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)"
BUCKET="anihpj-deployments"

# Create deployment package
zip -r deploy.zip . -x "*.git*" -x "__pycache__/*" -x "*.pyc"

# Upload to S3
aws s3 cp deploy.zip s3://$BUCKET/releases/$VERSION.zip

# Create version
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application-version \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --version-label $VERSION \
  --source-bundle S3Bucket=$BUCKET,S3Key=releases/$VERSION.zip \
  --process

echo "✓ Version $VERSION created"
✓ Version v20260113-100000 created
# Upload and create from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Packaged and uploaded to S3")
Example 3: Git-Based Versioning
#!/bin/bash
# Use git commit as version label
COMMIT=$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)
VERSION="git-$COMMIT"

aws elasticbeanstalk create-application-version \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --version-label $VERSION \
  --description "Commit: $(git log -1 --pretty=%B)" \
  --source-bundle S3Bucket=anihpj-deployments,S3Key=releases/$VERSION.zip

echo "✓ Created version for commit $COMMIT"
✓ Created version for commit abc1234
# Git versioning from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost commit = "abc1234" print(f"Version from git: {commit}")
Example 4: Auto-Create Application
#!/bin/bash
# Create app and first version in one command
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application-version \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost-new \
  --version-label v1.0.0 \
  --auto-create-application \
  --source-bundle S3Bucket=anihpj-deployments,S3Key=releases/v1.0.0.zip

echo "✓ App and version created"
✓ App and version created
# Auto-create from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"App auto-created with v1.0.0")
Example 5: Create with Tags
#!/bin/bash
# Create version with deployment tags
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application-version \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --version-label v2.2.0 \
  --description "Production release" \
  --source-bundle S3Bucket=anihpj-deployments,S3Key=releases/v2.2.0.zip \
  --tags Key=ReleaseType,Value=production Key=DeployedBy,Value=CI

echo "✓ Tagged version created"
✓ Tagged version created
# Version with tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost tags = {'ReleaseType': 'production'} print(f"Version tagged: {tags}")

describe-application-versions

Returns descriptions of application versions. List all versions or filter by labels to track deployment history.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
  [--application-name ] \
  [--version-labels ...] \
  [--max-records ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string): Filter by application name
  • --version-labels (list): Specific version labels
  • --max-records (integer): Max results (1-1000)
Return Value
  • ApplicationVersions (Array): List of versions
  • VersionLabel (String): Version identifier
  • Status (String): Processing status
Example 1: List All Versions
#!/bin/bash
# List all versions for application
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --region us-east-1
{ "ApplicationVersions": [ {"VersionLabel": "v2.1.0", "Status": "Processed", "DateCreated": "2026-01-13T10:00:00Z"}, {"VersionLabel": "v2.0.0", "Status": "Processed", "DateCreated": "2026-01-10T10:00:00Z"}, {"VersionLabel": "v1.0.0", "Status": "Processed", "DateCreated": "2026-01-01T10:00:00Z"} ] }
# List versions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost versions = ['v2.1.0', 'v2.0.0', 'v1.0.0'] print(f"Versions: {versions}")
Example 2: Get Specific Version
#!/bin/bash
# Get details for specific version
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --version-labels v2.1.0 \
  --region us-east-1
{ "ApplicationVersions": [ { "VersionLabel": "v2.1.0", "Description": "Added JobPost filtering", "SourceBundle": {"S3Bucket": "anihpj-deployments", "S3Key": "releases/v2.1.0.zip"}, "Status": "Processed" } ] }
# Specific version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version v2.1.0 details")
Example 3: List Version Labels Only
#!/bin/bash
# Get just version labels
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --query 'ApplicationVersions[].VersionLabel' \
  --output table
---------------- | VersionLabel | ---------------- | v2.1.0 | | v2.0.0 | | v1.0.0 | ----------------
# Version labels from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost labels = ['v2.1.0', 'v2.0.0', 'v1.0.0'] print(f"Labels: {labels}")
Example 4: Get Latest Version
#!/bin/bash
# Get most recent version
LATEST=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --max-records 1 \
  --query 'ApplicationVersions[0].VersionLabel' \
  --output text)

echo "Latest version: $LATEST"
Latest version: v2.1.0
# Latest version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost latest = "v2.1.0" print(f"Latest: {latest}")
Example 5: Check Version Status
#!/bin/bash
# Check processing status
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --version-labels v2.1.0 \
  --query 'ApplicationVersions[0].Status' \
  --output text
Processed
# Version status from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost status = "Processed" print(f"Status: {status}")

update-application-version

Updates the description for an existing application version. Use to add release notes or mark versions.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-version \
  --application-name  \
  --version-label  \
  [--description ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string): [Required] Application name
  • --version-label (string): [Required] Version to update
  • --description (string): New description
Return Value
  • ApplicationVersion (Object): Updated version info
Example 1: Update Description
#!/bin/bash
# Update version description
aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-version \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --version-label v2.1.0 \
  --description "Production release - JobPost filtering + Skills autocomplete" \
  --region us-east-1
{ "ApplicationVersion": { "VersionLabel": "v2.1.0", "Description": "Production release - JobPost filtering + Skills autocomplete", "DateUpdated": "2026-01-13T12:00:00.000Z" } }
# Update description from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version v2.1.0 description updated")
Example 2: Mark as Stable
#!/bin/bash
# Mark version as stable release
aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-version \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --version-label v2.0.0 \
  --description "[STABLE] Production-tested release"

echo "✓ Marked as stable"
✓ Marked as stable
# Mark stable from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version marked as STABLE")
Example 3: Add Release Notes
#!/bin/bash
# Add detailed release notes
aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-version \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --version-label v2.1.0 \
  --description "v2.1.0: Added Skills filtering, Location search, improved JobPost queries. Fixes: #123, #124"
{ "ApplicationVersion": { "VersionLabel": "v2.1.0", "Description": "v2.1.0: Added Skills filtering, Location search, improved JobPost queries. Fixes: #123, #124" } }
# Release notes from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Release notes added")
Example 4: Mark Deprecated
#!/bin/bash
# Mark old version as deprecated
aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-version \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --version-label v1.0.0 \
  --description "[DEPRECATED] Use v2.x - Security patches not applied"

echo "✓ Version deprecated"
✓ Version deprecated
# Mark deprecated from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Old version marked deprecated")
Example 5: Add Deploy Date
#!/bin/bash
# Add deployment timestamp
VERSION="v2.1.0"
DEPLOY_DATE=$(date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M")

aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-version \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --version-label $VERSION \
  --description "Deployed to production: $DEPLOY_DATE"
{ "ApplicationVersion": { "VersionLabel": "v2.1.0", "Description": "Deployed to production: 2026-01-13 14:30" } }
# Deploy date from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Deploy timestamp added")

delete-application-version

Deletes a specific application version. Optionally removes the source bundle from S3. Cannot delete versions deployed to environments.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application-version \
  --application-name  \
  --version-label  \
  [--delete-source-bundle] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string): [Required] Application name
  • --version-label (string): [Required] Version to delete
  • --delete-source-bundle (boolean): Also delete S3 source
Return Value
  • None: No output on success
Example 1: Delete Version
#!/bin/bash
# Delete old version (keep S3 source)
aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application-version \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --version-label v1.0.0 \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Version deleted"
✓ Version deleted
# Delete version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version v1.0.0 deleted")
Example 2: Delete with Source Bundle
#!/bin/bash
# Delete version and S3 source
aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application-version \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --version-label v1.0.0 \
  --delete-source-bundle

echo "✓ Version and S3 source deleted"
✓ Version and S3 source deleted
# Delete with S3 from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version and S3 bundle removed")
Example 3: Clean Old Versions
#!/bin/bash
# Delete versions older than 30 days
APP="anihpj-jobpost"
CUTOFF=$(date -d '30 days ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
  --application-name $APP \
  --query "ApplicationVersions[?DateCreated<'$CUTOFF'].VersionLabel" \
  --output text | while read VERSION; do
    aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application-version \
      --application-name $APP \
      --version-label $VERSION \
      --delete-source-bundle
    echo "Deleted: $VERSION"
done
Deleted: v0.9.0 Deleted: v0.8.0
# Clean old versions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost deleted = ['v0.9.0', 'v0.8.0'] print(f"Deleted: {deleted}")
Example 4: Keep Last N Versions
#!/bin/bash
# Keep only last 5 versions
APP="anihpj-jobpost"
KEEP=5

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
  --application-name $APP \
  --query "ApplicationVersions[$KEEP:].VersionLabel" \
  --output text | while read VERSION; do
    aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application-version \
      --application-name $APP \
      --version-label $VERSION \
      --delete-source-bundle 2>/dev/null
done

echo "✓ Kept last $KEEP versions"
✓ Kept last 5 versions
# Keep last N from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Kept last 5 versions only")
Example 5: Safe Delete Check
#!/bin/bash
# Check if version is deployed before deleting
APP="anihpj-jobpost"
VERSION="v1.5.0"

DEPLOYED=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
  --application-name $APP \
  --query "Environments[?VersionLabel=='$VERSION'].EnvironmentName" \
  --output text)

if [ -z "$DEPLOYED" ]; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application-version \
      --application-name $APP \
      --version-label $VERSION
    echo "✓ Deleted safely"
else
    echo "Cannot delete: deployed to $DEPLOYED"
fi
✓ Deleted safely
# Safe delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Safe deletion after deployment check")

create-environment

Creates a new environment to deploy your Django application. Configure instance type, scaling, load balancer, and environment variables.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk create-environment \
  --application-name  \
  --environment-name  \
  [--version-label ] \
  [--solution-stack-name ] \
  [--tier Name=,Type=] \
  [--option-settings ] \
  [--cname-prefix ] \
  [--tags ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string): [Required] Application name
  • --environment-name (string): [Required] Environment name
  • --version-label (string): Version to deploy
  • --solution-stack-name (string): Platform (e.g., Python 3.12)
  • --tier (structure): WebServer or Worker tier
  • --option-settings (list): Configuration options
  • --cname-prefix (string): URL prefix
Return Value
  • EnvironmentName (String): Environment name
  • EnvironmentId (String): Environment ID
  • Status (String): Launching status
  • CNAME (String): Environment URL
Example 1: Create Production Environment
#!/bin/bash
# Create production environment for Django
aws elasticbeanstalk create-environment \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --version-label v2.1.0 \
  --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
  --cname-prefix anihpj-jobpost-prod \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.medium \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:environment,OptionName=EnvironmentType,Value=LoadBalanced \
  --region us-east-1
{ "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-prod", "EnvironmentId": "e-abc123def", "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "VersionLabel": "v2.1.0", "Status": "Launching", "CNAME": "anihpj-jobpost-prod.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com" }
# Create prod env from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost env = "anihpj-prod" print(f"Environment launching: {env}")
Example 2: Create with Django Settings
#!/bin/bash
# Environment with Django environment variables
aws elasticbeanstalk create-environment \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --version-label v2.1.0 \
  --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE,Value=anihpj.settings.production \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=DEBUG,Value=False \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=ALLOWED_HOSTS,Value=.elasticbeanstalk.com
{ "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-prod", "Status": "Launching" }
# Django env vars from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Django settings configured")
Example 3: Create Worker Environment
#!/bin/bash
# Create worker tier for background jobs
aws elasticbeanstalk create-environment \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --environment-name anihpj-worker \
  --version-label v2.1.0 \
  --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
  --tier Name=Worker,Type=SQS/HTTP \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:sqsd,OptionName=WorkerQueueURL,Value=https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789012/anihpj-jobs
{ "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-worker", "Tier": {"Name": "Worker", "Type": "SQS/HTTP"}, "Status": "Launching" }
# Worker tier from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Worker environment for background jobs")
Example 4: Create Staging Environment
#!/bin/bash
# Create staging with smaller instance
aws elasticbeanstalk create-environment \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --environment-name anihpj-staging \
  --version-label v2.1.0 \
  --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
  --cname-prefix anihpj-staging \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.small \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:environment,OptionName=EnvironmentType,Value=SingleInstance \
  --tags Key=Environment,Value=staging

echo "✓ Staging environment created"
✓ Staging environment created
# Staging env from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Staging SingleInstance created")
Example 5: Create with RDS Database
#!/bin/bash
# Environment connected to RDS
aws elasticbeanstalk create-environment \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --version-label v2.1.0 \
  --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=RDS_HOSTNAME,Value=anihpj-db.abc123.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=RDS_DB_NAME,Value=anihpj_jobpost \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=RDS_USERNAME,Value=anihpj_admin
{ "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-prod", "Status": "Launching" }
# RDS connected from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost db_count = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"Connected to RDS: {db_count} jobs")

describe-environments

Returns descriptions of existing environments. Get status, health, URL, and deployed version for monitoring and deployment verification.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
  [--application-name ] \
  [--environment-names ...] \
  [--environment-ids ...] \
  [--include-deleted] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string): Filter by application
  • --environment-names (list): Specific environments
  • --environment-ids (list): Filter by IDs
  • --include-deleted (boolean): Include terminated
Return Value
  • Environments (Array): List of environments
  • Status (String): Ready, Launching, Updating, Terminating
  • Health (String): Green, Yellow, Red, Grey
  • CNAME (String): Environment URL
Example 1: List All Environments
#!/bin/bash
# List all environments for application
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Environments": [ { "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-prod", "Status": "Ready", "Health": "Green", "VersionLabel": "v2.1.0", "CNAME": "anihpj-jobpost-prod.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com" }, { "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-staging", "Status": "Ready", "Health": "Green", "VersionLabel": "v2.2.0-beta" } ] }
# List environments from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost envs = ['anihpj-prod', 'anihpj-staging'] print(f"Environments: {envs}")
Example 2: Get Environment URL
#!/bin/bash
# Get production URL
URL=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
  --environment-names anihpj-prod \
  --query 'Environments[0].CNAME' \
  --output text)

echo "Production URL: http://$URL"
Production URL: http://anihpj-jobpost-prod.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com
# Get URL from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost url = "anihpj-jobpost-prod.elasticbeanstalk.com" print(f"URL: {url}")
Example 3: Check Environment Health
#!/bin/bash
# Get health status
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
  --environment-names anihpj-prod \
  --query 'Environments[0].[EnvironmentName,Status,Health]' \
  --output table
----------------------------------- | anihpj-prod | Ready | Green | -----------------------------------
# Health check from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost health = "Green" print(f"Health: {health}")
Example 4: Get Deployed Version
#!/bin/bash
# Check which version is deployed
VERSION=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
  --environment-names anihpj-prod \
  --query 'Environments[0].VersionLabel' \
  --output text)

echo "Deployed version: $VERSION"
Deployed version: v2.1.0
# Deployed version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost version = "v2.1.0" print(f"Running: {version}")
Example 5: Monitor Deployment Status
#!/bin/bash
# Wait for environment to be ready
ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

while true; do
    STATUS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
      --environment-names $ENV_NAME \
      --query 'Environments[0].Status' \
      --output text)
    
    echo "Status: $STATUS"
    
    if [ "$STATUS" == "Ready" ]; then
        echo "✓ Environment ready!"
        break
    fi
    sleep 30
done
Status: Updating Status: Updating Status: Ready ✓ Environment ready!
# Monitor status from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment ready")

update-environment

Updates environment configuration or deploys a new application version. Use for deployments, scaling changes, and configuration updates.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk update-environment \
  --environment-name  \
  [--version-label ] \
  [--option-settings ] \
  [--options-to-remove ] \
  [--description ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string): [Required] Environment to update
  • --version-label (string): Deploy this version
  • --option-settings (list): Configuration changes
  • --options-to-remove (list): Settings to remove
Return Value
  • EnvironmentName (String): Environment name
  • Status (String): Updating status
  • VersionLabel (String): New version
Example 1: Deploy New Version
#!/bin/bash
# Deploy new version to production
aws elasticbeanstalk update-environment \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --version-label v2.2.0 \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Deployment started"
{ "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-prod", "VersionLabel": "v2.2.0", "Status": "Updating" } ✓ Deployment started
# Deploy version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Deploying v2.2.0 to production")
Example 2: Update Instance Type
#!/bin/bash
# Scale up to larger instance
aws elasticbeanstalk update-environment \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.large

echo "✓ Scaling to t3.large"
{ "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-prod", "Status": "Updating" } ✓ Scaling to t3.large
# Scale instance from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Scaling to t3.large")
Example 3: Update Environment Variables
#!/bin/bash
# Update Django environment variables
aws elasticbeanstalk update-environment \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=DEBUG,Value=False \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=SECRET_KEY,Value=new-secret-key-here
{ "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-prod", "Status": "Updating" }
# Update env vars from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment variables updated")
Example 4: Configure Auto Scaling
#!/bin/bash
# Set auto-scaling min/max instances
aws elasticbeanstalk update-environment \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MinSize,Value=2 \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MaxSize,Value=10

echo "✓ Auto-scaling configured: 2-10 instances"
✓ Auto-scaling configured: 2-10 instances
# Auto-scaling from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Auto-scaling: 2-10 instances")
Example 5: Deploy with Rolling Update
#!/bin/bash
# Deploy with rolling update (no downtime)
aws elasticbeanstalk update-environment \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --version-label v2.2.0 \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:command,OptionName=DeploymentPolicy,Value=Rolling \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:command,OptionName=BatchSize,Value=30

echo "✓ Rolling deployment: 30% at a time"
✓ Rolling deployment: 30% at a time
# Rolling deploy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Rolling update: zero downtime")

terminate-environment

Terminates the specified environment and releases all associated AWS resources. Use for cleanup or decommissioning environments.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk terminate-environment \
  --environment-name  \
  [--terminate-resources] \
  [--force-terminate] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string): [Required] Environment to terminate
  • --terminate-resources (boolean): Delete AWS resources
  • --force-terminate (boolean): Force immediate termination
Return Value
  • EnvironmentName (String): Environment name
  • Status (String): Terminating
Example 1: Terminate Environment
#!/bin/bash
# Terminate staging environment
aws elasticbeanstalk terminate-environment \
  --environment-name anihpj-staging \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Termination started"
{ "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-staging", "Status": "Terminating", "DateUpdated": "2026-01-13T14:00:00.000Z" } ✓ Termination started
# Terminate env from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Staging environment terminating")
Example 2: Force Terminate
#!/bin/bash
# Force immediate termination
aws elasticbeanstalk terminate-environment \
  --environment-name anihpj-test \
  --force-terminate

echo "✓ Force terminated"
✓ Force terminated
# Force terminate from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Immediate termination")
Example 3: Wait for Termination
#!/bin/bash
# Terminate and wait for completion
ENV_NAME="anihpj-staging"

aws elasticbeanstalk terminate-environment \
  --environment-name $ENV_NAME

echo "Waiting for termination..."
aws elasticbeanstalk wait environment-terminated \
  --environment-names $ENV_NAME

echo "✓ Environment terminated"
Waiting for termination... ✓ Environment terminated
# Wait for termination from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Termination complete")
Example 4: Terminate Old Environment
#!/bin/bash
# Terminate old environment after blue-green deploy
OLD_ENV="anihpj-prod-blue"

# Verify new environment is healthy first
HEALTH=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
  --environment-names anihpj-prod-green \
  --query 'Environments[0].Health' \
  --output text)

if [ "$HEALTH" == "Green" ]; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk terminate-environment \
      --environment-name $OLD_ENV
    echo "✓ Old environment terminated"
else
    echo "New env not healthy, keeping old"
fi
✓ Old environment terminated
# Blue-green cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Old blue environment removed")
Example 5: Cleanup All Test Environments
#!/bin/bash
# Terminate all test environments
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --query "Environments[?contains(EnvironmentName,'test')].EnvironmentName" \
  --output text | while read ENV; do
    aws elasticbeanstalk terminate-environment \
      --environment-name $ENV
    echo "Terminating: $ENV"
done
Terminating: anihpj-test-1 Terminating: anihpj-test-2
# Cleanup tests from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost terminated = ['test-1', 'test-2'] print(f"Terminated: {terminated}")

restart-app-server

Restarts the application server running on each instance. Quick way to apply configuration changes or recover from issues without full redeployment.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk restart-app-server \
  --environment-name  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string): [Required] Environment to restart
Return Value
  • None: No output on success
Example 1: Restart Production
#!/bin/bash
# Restart Django app servers
aws elasticbeanstalk restart-app-server \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ App servers restarting"
✓ App servers restarting
# Restart servers from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"App servers restarting")
Example 2: Restart After Config Change
#!/bin/bash
# Restart to pick up new environment variables
ENV="anihpj-prod"

# Update env var
aws elasticbeanstalk update-environment \
  --environment-name $ENV \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=CACHE_TTL,Value=3600

# Restart to apply immediately
aws elasticbeanstalk restart-app-server \
  --environment-name $ENV

echo "✓ Config applied with restart"
✓ Config applied with restart
# Config restart from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Config applied, servers restarted")
Example 3: Recovery Restart
#!/bin/bash
# Restart to recover from memory issues
ENV="anihpj-prod"

# Check health first
HEALTH=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
  --environment-name $ENV \
  --attribute-names HealthStatus \
  --query 'HealthStatus' \
  --output text)

if [ "$HEALTH" != "Ok" ]; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk restart-app-server \
      --environment-name $ENV
    echo "✓ Recovery restart initiated"
fi
✓ Recovery restart initiated
# Recovery restart from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Recovery restart for unhealthy env")
Example 4: Restart All Environments
#!/bin/bash
# Restart all environments in application
for ENV in $(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --query 'Environments[?Status==`Ready`].EnvironmentName' \
  --output text); do
    aws elasticbeanstalk restart-app-server \
      --environment-name $ENV
    echo "Restarted: $ENV"
done
Restarted: anihpj-prod Restarted: anihpj-staging
# Restart all from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All environments restarted")
Example 5: Restart with Verification
#!/bin/bash
# Restart and verify health
ENV="anihpj-prod"

aws elasticbeanstalk restart-app-server \
  --environment-name $ENV

echo "Waiting for restart..."
sleep 60

HEALTH=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
  --environment-name $ENV \
  --query 'HealthStatus' \
  --output text)

echo "Health after restart: $HEALTH"
Waiting for restart... Health after restart: Ok
# Verify restart from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Health verified after restart")

rebuild-environment

Deletes and recreates all AWS resources in the environment. Use when deep infrastructure issues require a fresh start while keeping configuration.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk rebuild-environment \
  --environment-name  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string): [Required] Environment to rebuild
Return Value
  • None: No output on success
Example 1: Rebuild Environment
#!/bin/bash
# Full rebuild of environment
aws elasticbeanstalk rebuild-environment \
  --environment-name anihpj-staging \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Rebuild started (5-10 minutes)"
✓ Rebuild started (5-10 minutes)
# Rebuild env from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment rebuilding")
Example 2: Rebuild After Corruption
#!/bin/bash
# Rebuild when environment is corrupted
ENV="anihpj-prod"

echo "⚠ Warning: This will cause downtime"
read -p "Continue? (y/n): " CONFIRM

if [ "$CONFIRM" == "y" ]; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk rebuild-environment \
      --environment-name $ENV
    echo "✓ Rebuild initiated"
fi
⚠ Warning: This will cause downtime Continue? (y/n): y ✓ Rebuild initiated
# Rebuild corrupted from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Corrupted environment rebuilding")
Example 3: Rebuild and Monitor
#!/bin/bash
# Rebuild with status monitoring
ENV="anihpj-staging"

aws elasticbeanstalk rebuild-environment \
  --environment-name $ENV

while true; do
    STATUS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
      --environment-names $ENV \
      --query 'Environments[0].Status' \
      --output text)
    echo "Status: $STATUS"
    [ "$STATUS" == "Ready" ] && break
    sleep 30
done

echo "✓ Rebuild complete"
Status: Updating Status: Updating Status: Ready ✓ Rebuild complete
# Monitor rebuild from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Rebuild complete")
Example 4: Rebuild with Health Check
#!/bin/bash
# Rebuild only if unhealthy
ENV="anihpj-prod"

HEALTH=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
  --environment-name $ENV \
  --query 'HealthStatus' \
  --output text)

if [ "$HEALTH" == "Severe" ] || [ "$HEALTH" == "Degraded" ]; then
    echo "Health: $HEALTH - Rebuilding..."
    aws elasticbeanstalk rebuild-environment \
      --environment-name $ENV
else
    echo "Health: $HEALTH - No rebuild needed"
fi
Health: Severe - Rebuilding...
# Health-based rebuild from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Rebuild triggered by health")
Example 5: Scheduled Rebuild
#!/bin/bash
# Rebuild non-production during maintenance window
ENV="anihpj-staging"
HOUR=$(date +%H)

# Only rebuild between 2-4 AM
if [ "$HOUR" -ge 2 ] && [ "$HOUR" -lt 4 ]; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk rebuild-environment \
      --environment-name $ENV
    echo "✓ Maintenance rebuild started"
else
    echo "Outside maintenance window"
fi
✓ Maintenance rebuild started
# Maintenance rebuild from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Scheduled maintenance rebuild")

swap-environment-cnames

Swaps the CNAMEs between two environments for blue-green deployments. Instantly redirect traffic from one environment to another with zero downtime.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk swap-environment-cnames \
  --source-environment-name  \
  --destination-environment-name  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --source-environment-name (string): [Required] First environment
  • --destination-environment-name (string): [Required] Second environment
Return Value
  • None: No output on success
Example 1: Blue-Green Swap
#!/bin/bash
# Swap production traffic to new environment
aws elasticbeanstalk swap-environment-cnames \
  --source-environment-name anihpj-prod-blue \
  --destination-environment-name anihpj-prod-green \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Traffic swapped to green environment"
✓ Traffic swapped to green environment
# Blue-green swap from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Production now on green")
Example 2: Promote Staging to Prod
#!/bin/bash
# Swap staging URL with production
aws elasticbeanstalk swap-environment-cnames \
  --source-environment-name anihpj-staging \
  --destination-environment-name anihpj-prod

echo "✓ Staging promoted to production URL"
✓ Staging promoted to production URL
# Promote staging from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Staging now serving production")
Example 3: Rollback with Swap
#!/bin/bash
# Quick rollback by swapping back
OLD_ENV="anihpj-prod-v1"
NEW_ENV="anihpj-prod-v2"

echo "Rolling back to previous version..."
aws elasticbeanstalk swap-environment-cnames \
  --source-environment-name $NEW_ENV \
  --destination-environment-name $OLD_ENV

echo "✓ Rollback complete"
Rolling back to previous version... ✓ Rollback complete
# Rollback swap from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Rolled back to previous version")
Example 4: Safe Swap with Health Check
#!/bin/bash
# Only swap if new environment is healthy
NEW_ENV="anihpj-prod-green"
OLD_ENV="anihpj-prod-blue"

HEALTH=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
  --environment-name $NEW_ENV \
  --query 'HealthStatus' \
  --output text)

if [ "$HEALTH" == "Ok" ]; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk swap-environment-cnames \
      --source-environment-name $OLD_ENV \
      --destination-environment-name $NEW_ENV
    echo "✓ Swap complete"
else
    echo "✗ New env unhealthy: $HEALTH"
fi
✓ Swap complete
# Safe swap from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Swapped after health verification")
Example 5: Verify Swap
#!/bin/bash
# Swap and verify URLs changed
ENV1="anihpj-prod-blue"
ENV2="anihpj-prod-green"

# Get URLs before
URL1_BEFORE=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
  --environment-names $ENV1 --query 'Environments[0].CNAME' --output text)

# Swap
aws elasticbeanstalk swap-environment-cnames \
  --source-environment-name $ENV1 \
  --destination-environment-name $ENV2

# Get URLs after
URL1_AFTER=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
  --environment-names $ENV1 --query 'Environments[0].CNAME' --output text)

echo "Before: $URL1_BEFORE"
echo "After: $URL1_AFTER"
Before: anihpj-prod.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com After: anihpj-staging.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com
# Verify swap from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"URLs successfully swapped")

describe-environment-health

Returns detailed health information for the environment including status, causes, and request statistics. Essential for monitoring and alerting.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
  --environment-name  \
  [--attribute-names ...] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string): [Required] Environment name
  • --attribute-names (list): Attributes: Status, Color, Causes, ApplicationMetrics, InstancesHealth, All, HealthStatus, RefreshedAt
Return Value
  • HealthStatus (String): Ok, Warning, Degraded, Severe
  • Color (String): Green, Yellow, Red, Grey
  • Causes (Array): Health issue descriptions
  • ApplicationMetrics (Object): Request statistics
Example 1: Get Health Status
#!/bin/bash
# Get environment health status
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --attribute-names HealthStatus Color \
  --region us-east-1
{ "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-prod", "HealthStatus": "Ok", "Color": "Green", "RefreshedAt": "2026-01-13T14:00:00.000Z" }
# Health status from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost health = "Ok" color = "Green" print(f"Health: {health}, Color: {color}")
Example 2: Get All Health Data
#!/bin/bash
# Get comprehensive health info
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --attribute-names All
{ "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-prod", "HealthStatus": "Ok", "Color": "Green", "Causes": [], "ApplicationMetrics": { "Duration": 10, "RequestCount": 1250, "StatusCodes": {"Status2xx": 1200, "Status4xx": 45, "Status5xx": 5}, "Latency": {"P99": 0.5, "P90": 0.2, "P50": 0.05} }, "InstancesHealth": {"Ok": 3, "Warning": 0, "Degraded": 0} }
# All health data from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost requests = 1250 print(f"Requests: {requests}, Health: Ok")
Example 3: Check for Issues
#!/bin/bash
# Get health causes (issues)
CAUSES=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --attribute-names Causes \
  --query 'Causes' \
  --output text)

if [ -n "$CAUSES" ]; then
    echo "⚠ Issues found:"
    echo "$CAUSES"
else
    echo "✓ No health issues"
fi
✓ No health issues
# Check issues from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost issues = [] print(f"Issues: {issues}")
Example 4: Get Request Metrics
#!/bin/bash
# Get application request metrics
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --attribute-names ApplicationMetrics \
  --query 'ApplicationMetrics.{Requests:RequestCount,P99Latency:Latency.P99,Errors:StatusCodes.Status5xx}'
{ "Requests": 1250, "P99Latency": 0.5, "Errors": 5 }
# Request metrics from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost metrics = {'requests': 1250, 'p99': 0.5} print(f"Metrics: {metrics}")
Example 5: Health Monitoring Script
#!/bin/bash
# Continuous health monitoring
ENV="anihpj-prod"

while true; do
    HEALTH=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
      --environment-name $ENV \
      --attribute-names HealthStatus Color \
      --query '[HealthStatus,Color]' \
      --output text)
    
    echo "$(date): $HEALTH"
    
    if echo "$HEALTH" | grep -q "Severe\|Red"; then
        echo "⚠ ALERT: Environment unhealthy!"
    fi
    
    sleep 60
done
2026-01-13 14:00:00: Ok Green 2026-01-13 14:01:00: Ok Green
# Health monitoring from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Continuous health monitoring")

describe-instances-health

Returns health information for individual EC2 instances in the environment. Identify problematic instances for troubleshooting.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-instances-health \
  --environment-name  \
  [--attribute-names ...] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string): [Required] Environment name
  • --attribute-names (list): Attributes: HealthStatus, Color, Causes, ApplicationMetrics, System, All
Return Value
  • InstanceHealthList (Array): Per-instance health data
  • InstanceId (String): EC2 instance ID
  • HealthStatus (String): Instance health
Example 1: List Instance Health
#!/bin/bash
# Get health for all instances
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-instances-health \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --attribute-names All \
  --region us-east-1
{ "InstanceHealthList": [ {"InstanceId": "i-0abc123", "HealthStatus": "Ok", "Color": "Green"}, {"InstanceId": "i-0def456", "HealthStatus": "Ok", "Color": "Green"}, {"InstanceId": "i-0ghi789", "HealthStatus": "Ok", "Color": "Green"} ], "RefreshedAt": "2026-01-13T14:00:00.000Z" }
# Instance health from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost instances = 3 print(f"Healthy instances: {instances}")
Example 2: Get Instance IDs
#!/bin/bash
# List all instance IDs in environment
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-instances-health \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --query 'InstanceHealthList[].InstanceId' \
  --output table
--------------- | InstanceId | --------------- | i-0abc123 | | i-0def456 | | i-0ghi789 | ---------------
# Instance IDs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost ids = ['i-0abc123', 'i-0def456', 'i-0ghi789'] print(f"Instances: {ids}")
Example 3: Find Unhealthy Instances
#!/bin/bash
# Find instances with issues
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-instances-health \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --attribute-names HealthStatus \
  --query "InstanceHealthList[?HealthStatus!='Ok'].[InstanceId,HealthStatus]" \
  --output table
----------------------------- | InstanceId | HealthStatus | ----------------------------- | i-0problem | Degraded | -----------------------------
# Unhealthy instances from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost unhealthy = ['i-0problem'] print(f"Unhealthy: {unhealthy}")
Example 4: Get Instance Metrics
#!/bin/bash
# Get CPU and request metrics per instance
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-instances-health \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --attribute-names ApplicationMetrics System \
  --query 'InstanceHealthList[].{ID:InstanceId,CPU:System.CPUUtilization.User,Requests:ApplicationMetrics.RequestCount}'
[ {"ID": "i-0abc123", "CPU": 25.5, "Requests": 450}, {"ID": "i-0def456", "CPU": 30.2, "Requests": 410}, {"ID": "i-0ghi789", "CPU": 22.8, "Requests": 390} ]
# Instance metrics from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Per-instance CPU and requests")
Example 5: Count Healthy vs Unhealthy
#!/bin/bash
# Summarize instance health
ENV="anihpj-prod"

HEALTHY=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-instances-health \
  --environment-name $ENV \
  --query "length(InstanceHealthList[?HealthStatus=='Ok'])" \
  --output text)

TOTAL=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-instances-health \
  --environment-name $ENV \
  --query "length(InstanceHealthList)" \
  --output text)

echo "Healthy: $HEALTHY / $TOTAL instances"
Healthy: 3 / 3 instances
# Health count from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost healthy = 3 total = 3 print(f"Healthy: {healthy}/{total}")

describe-environment-resources

Returns AWS resources associated with the environment including Auto Scaling groups, load balancers, instances, and queues.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-resources \
  --environment-name  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string): [Required] Environment name
Return Value
  • EnvironmentResources (Object): Resource details
  • AutoScalingGroups (Array): ASG names
  • Instances (Array): EC2 instance IDs
  • LoadBalancers (Array): ELB names
Example 1: List All Resources
#!/bin/bash
# Get all AWS resources in environment
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-resources \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --region us-east-1
{ "EnvironmentResources": { "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-prod", "AutoScalingGroups": [{"Name": "awseb-e-abc123-stack-AWSEBAutoScalingGroup"}], "Instances": [{"Id": "i-0abc123"}, {"Id": "i-0def456"}], "LoadBalancers": [{"Name": "awseb-e-a-AWSEBLoa-123ABC"}], "LaunchConfigurations": [{"Name": "awseb-e-abc123-stack"}] } }
# All resources from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost resources = {'instances': 2, 'load_balancers': 1} print(f"Resources: {resources}")
Example 2: Get Load Balancer
#!/bin/bash
# Get load balancer name
LB=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-resources \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --query 'EnvironmentResources.LoadBalancers[0].Name' \
  --output text)

echo "Load Balancer: $LB"
Load Balancer: awseb-e-a-AWSEBLoa-123ABC
# Load balancer from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost lb = "awseb-e-a-AWSEBLoa-123ABC" print(f"LB: {lb}")
Example 3: Get Auto Scaling Group
#!/bin/bash
# Get ASG for scaling operations
ASG=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-resources \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --query 'EnvironmentResources.AutoScalingGroups[0].Name' \
  --output text)

echo "Auto Scaling Group: $ASG"

# Now can use with autoscaling commands
aws autoscaling describe-auto-scaling-groups \
  --auto-scaling-group-names $ASG
Auto Scaling Group: awseb-e-abc123-stack-AWSEBAutoScalingGroup
# ASG name from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost asg = "awseb-stack-ASG" print(f"ASG: {asg}")
Example 4: List Instance IDs
#!/bin/bash
# Get EC2 instance IDs for SSH or debugging
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-resources \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --query 'EnvironmentResources.Instances[].Id' \
  --output table
------------- | Id | ------------- | i-0abc123 | | i-0def456 | -------------
# Instance IDs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost instances = ['i-0abc123', 'i-0def456'] print(f"Instances: {instances}")
Example 5: Get Queue URL (Worker)
#!/bin/bash
# Get SQS queue for worker environment
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-resources \
  --environment-name anihpj-worker \
  --query 'EnvironmentResources.Queues[0].URL' \
  --output text
https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789012/awseb-e-worker-queue
# Worker queue from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost queue = "awseb-e-worker-queue" print(f"SQS Queue: {queue}")

describe-events

Returns events for the environment including deployments, configuration changes, and health transitions. Essential for debugging and audit trails.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-events \
  [--application-name ] \
  [--environment-name ] \
  [--severity ] \
  [--start-time ] \
  [--end-time ] \
  [--max-records ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string): Filter by environment
  • --severity (string): TRACE, DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR, FATAL
  • --start-time (timestamp): Events after this time
  • --max-records (integer): Max events to return
Return Value
  • Events (Array): List of events
  • Message (String): Event description
  • Severity (String): Event severity level
Example 1: Get Recent Events
#!/bin/bash
# Get recent events for environment
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-events \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --max-records 10 \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Events": [ { "EventDate": "2026-01-13T14:00:00.000Z", "Message": "Environment health has transitioned to Ok", "Severity": "INFO" }, { "EventDate": "2026-01-13T13:55:00.000Z", "Message": "New application version was deployed", "Severity": "INFO" } ] }
# Recent events from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost events = ['Health Ok', 'Version deployed'] print(f"Events: {events}")
Example 2: Get Errors Only
#!/bin/bash
# Filter for error events
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-events \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --severity ERROR \
  --max-records 20
{ "Events": [ { "EventDate": "2026-01-12T10:00:00.000Z", "Message": "Instance deployment failed", "Severity": "ERROR" } ] }
# Error events from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost errors = ['Instance deployment failed'] print(f"Errors: {errors}")
Example 3: Events in Time Range
#!/bin/bash
# Get events from last hour
START=$(date -d '1 hour ago' --iso-8601=seconds)

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-events \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --start-time $START \
  --query 'Events[].[EventDate,Severity,Message]' \
  --output table
---------------------------------------------------------- | EventDate | Severity | Message | ---------------------------------------------------------- | 2026-01-13T14:00:00Z | INFO | Health Ok | | 2026-01-13T13:55:00Z | INFO | Deployed v2.1.0 | ----------------------------------------------------------
# Time range events from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Events from last hour")
Example 4: Deployment Events
#!/bin/bash
# Track deployment progress
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-events \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --max-records 50 \
  --query "Events[?contains(Message,'deploy') || contains(Message,'version')].[EventDate,Message]" \
  --output table
------------------------------------------------------ | EventDate | Message | ------------------------------------------------------ | 2026-01-13T13:55:00Z | New version deployed | | 2026-01-13T13:50:00Z | Deploying version v2.1.0 | ------------------------------------------------------
# Deployment events from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Deployment history tracked")
Example 5: Monitor Events Live
#!/bin/bash
# Tail events during deployment
ENV="anihpj-prod"
LAST_TIME=$(date --iso-8601=seconds)

while true; do
    aws elasticbeanstalk describe-events \
      --environment-name $ENV \
      --start-time $LAST_TIME \
      --query 'Events[].Message' \
      --output text
    
    LAST_TIME=$(date --iso-8601=seconds)
    sleep 10
done
Deploying new version v2.1.0 Instance deployment started Instance deployment completed Environment health Ok
# Live events from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Live event monitoring")

create-configuration-template

Creates a reusable configuration template from an existing environment or from scratch. Use to standardize environment settings across deployments.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk create-configuration-template \
  --application-name  \
  --template-name  \
  [--solution-stack-name ] \
  [--source-configuration ApplicationName=<>,TemplateName=<>] \
  [--environment-id ] \
  [--description ] \
  [--option-settings ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string): [Required] Application name
  • --template-name (string): [Required] Template name
  • --solution-stack-name (string): Platform stack
  • --environment-id (string): Copy from environment
  • --option-settings (list): Configuration options
Return Value
  • TemplateName (String): Template name
  • SolutionStackName (String): Platform
Example 1: Create from Environment
#!/bin/bash
# Create template from production environment
ENV_ID=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
  --environment-names anihpj-prod \
  --query 'Environments[0].EnvironmentId' \
  --output text)

aws elasticbeanstalk create-configuration-template \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --template-name prod-config-v1 \
  --environment-id $ENV_ID \
  --description "Production configuration snapshot"
{ "TemplateName": "prod-config-v1", "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "Description": "Production configuration snapshot", "SolutionStackName": "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" }
# Template from env from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Template created from prod")
Example 2: Create Django Template
#!/bin/bash
# Create standard Django template
aws elasticbeanstalk create-configuration-template \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --template-name django-standard \
  --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.medium \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE,Value=anihpj.settings.production
{ "TemplateName": "django-standard", "SolutionStackName": "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" }
# Django template from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Standard Django template created")
Example 3: High-Availability Template
#!/bin/bash
# Create HA production template
aws elasticbeanstalk create-configuration-template \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --template-name ha-production \
  --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:environment,OptionName=EnvironmentType,Value=LoadBalanced \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MinSize,Value=2 \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MaxSize,Value=10 \
  --description "HA production with auto-scaling"
{ "TemplateName": "ha-production", "Description": "HA production with auto-scaling" }
# HA template from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"High-availability template created")
Example 4: Development Template
#!/bin/bash
# Create low-cost dev template
aws elasticbeanstalk create-configuration-template \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --template-name dev-single \
  --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:environment,OptionName=EnvironmentType,Value=SingleInstance \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.micro \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=DEBUG,Value=True
{ "TemplateName": "dev-single", "Description": "Development single instance" }
# Dev template from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Low-cost dev template")
Example 5: Copy Existing Template
#!/bin/bash
# Create new template based on existing
aws elasticbeanstalk create-configuration-template \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --template-name prod-config-v2 \
  --source-configuration ApplicationName=anihpj-jobpost,TemplateName=prod-config-v1 \
  --description "Updated production config"

echo "✓ Template copied and ready for modifications"
✓ Template copied and ready for modifications
# Copy template from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Template copied from v1 to v2")

describe-configuration-settings

Returns configuration settings for an environment or configuration template. Shows all configurable options and their current values.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-settings \
  --application-name  \
  [--template-name ] \
  [--environment-name ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string): [Required] Application name
  • --template-name (string): Template to describe (mutually exclusive with environment-name)
  • --environment-name (string): Environment to describe
Return Value
  • ConfigurationSettings (Array): List of settings
  • OptionSettings (Array): Namespace/option/value tuples
Example 1: Get Environment Settings
#!/bin/bash
# Get current environment configuration
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-settings \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --query 'ConfigurationSettings[0].OptionSettings' \
  --output table
{ "ConfigurationSettings": [{ "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-prod", "OptionSettings": [ {"Namespace": "aws:elasticbeanstalk:environment", "OptionName": "EnvironmentType", "Value": "LoadBalanced"}, {"Namespace": "aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration", "OptionName": "InstanceType", "Value": "t3.medium"} ] }] }
# Environment settings from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Current settings retrieved")
Example 2: Get Template Settings
#!/bin/bash
# Get template configuration
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-settings \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --template-name prod-config-v1
{ "ConfigurationSettings": [{ "TemplateName": "prod-config-v1", "SolutionStackName": "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" }] }
# Template settings from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Template config retrieved")
Example 3: Extract Instance Type
#!/bin/bash
# Get current instance type
INSTANCE_TYPE=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-settings \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --query "ConfigurationSettings[0].OptionSettings[?OptionName=='InstanceType'].Value" \
  --output text)

echo "Instance Type: $INSTANCE_TYPE"
Instance Type: t3.medium
# Instance type from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Running on t3.medium")
Example 4: Get Environment Variables
#!/bin/bash
# Get app environment variables
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-settings \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --query "ConfigurationSettings[0].OptionSettings[?Namespace=='aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment']" \
  --output table
--------------------------------------------------- | OptionSettings | +---------------+---------------------+------------+ | Namespace | OptionName | Value | +---------------+---------------------+------------+ | ...environment| DJANGO_SETTINGS_... | anihpj... | | ...environment| DATABASE_URL | postgres://| +---------------+---------------------+------------+
# Env vars from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment vars listed")
Example 5: Export Settings to JSON
#!/bin/bash
# Export full config for backup
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-settings \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  > prod-config-backup.json

echo "✓ Configuration exported to prod-config-backup.json"
✓ Configuration exported to prod-config-backup.json
# Export config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Config backed up to JSON")

describe-configuration-options

Lists all available configuration options for a solution stack or saved configuration. Use to discover what settings can be modified.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-options \
  [--application-name ] \
  [--template-name ] \
  [--environment-name ] \
  [--solution-stack-name ] \
  [--options ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string): Application name
  • --solution-stack-name (string): Platform stack to query
  • --environment-name (string): Environment name
  • --options (list): Specific options to describe
Return Value
  • Options (Array): Available configuration options
  • SolutionStackName (String): Platform name
Example 1: List All Options for Platform
#!/bin/bash
# Get all options for Python platform
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-options \
  --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
  --query 'Options[].{Namespace:Namespace,Name:Name}' \
  --output table
{ "SolutionStackName": "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12", "Options": [ {"Namespace": "aws:autoscaling:asg", "Name": "MinSize", "DefaultValue": "1"}, {"Namespace": "aws:autoscaling:asg", "Name": "MaxSize", "DefaultValue": "4"}, {"Namespace": "aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration", "Name": "InstanceType"} ] }
# Platform options from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Available options listed")
Example 2: Get Instance Type Options
#!/bin/bash
# Get valid instance types
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-options \
  --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
  --options Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType \
  --query 'Options[0].ValueOptions'
[ "t3.micro", "t3.small", "t3.medium", "t3.large", "t3.xlarge", "m5.large", "m5.xlarge" ]
# Instance types from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Valid instance types")
Example 3: Get Autoscaling Options
#!/bin/bash
# Get autoscaling config options
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-options \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --query "Options[?Namespace=='aws:autoscaling:asg']"
[ {"Namespace": "aws:autoscaling:asg", "Name": "MinSize", "MinValue": 1, "MaxValue": 10000}, {"Namespace": "aws:autoscaling:asg", "Name": "MaxSize", "MinValue": 1, "MaxValue": 10000}, {"Namespace": "aws:autoscaling:asg", "Name": "Cooldown", "DefaultValue": "360"} ]
# ASG options from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Autoscaling options listed")
Example 4: Get Environment Variable Options
#!/bin/bash
# Check environment variable namespace
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-options \
  --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
  --query "Options[?Namespace=='aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment']"
[ { "Namespace": "aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment", "Name": "*", "UserDefined": true, "ValueType": "Scalar" } ]
# Env var options from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Custom env vars supported")
Example 5: List All Namespaces
#!/bin/bash
# Get unique namespaces
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-options \
  --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
  --query 'Options[].Namespace' \
  --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | sort -u
aws:autoscaling:asg aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration aws:autoscaling:trigger aws:elasticbeanstalk:application aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment aws:elasticbeanstalk:environment aws:elb:loadbalancer
# Namespaces from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All config namespaces")

update-configuration-template

Modifies an existing configuration template with new option settings. Use to update templates without recreating them.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk update-configuration-template \
  --application-name  \
  --template-name  \
  [--description ] \
  [--option-settings ] \
  [--options-to-remove ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string): [Required] Application name
  • --template-name (string): [Required] Template to update
  • --option-settings (list): Options to add or change
  • --options-to-remove (list): Options to remove
Return Value
  • TemplateName (String): Updated template name
  • DateUpdated (Timestamp): Update time
Example 1: Update Instance Type
#!/bin/bash
# Upgrade instance type in template
aws elasticbeanstalk update-configuration-template \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --template-name prod-config-v1 \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.large
{ "TemplateName": "prod-config-v1", "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "DateUpdated": "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z" }
# Update instance from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Template updated to t3.large")
Example 2: Update Autoscaling Limits
#!/bin/bash
# Scale up autoscaling limits
aws elasticbeanstalk update-configuration-template \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --template-name ha-production \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MinSize,Value=3 \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MaxSize,Value=15
{ "TemplateName": "ha-production", "Description": "Updated autoscaling limits" }
# Update ASG from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"ASG: min=3, max=15")
Example 3: Add Environment Variable
#!/bin/bash
# Add new Django setting
aws elasticbeanstalk update-configuration-template \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --template-name prod-config-v1 \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=CACHE_TTL,Value=3600
{ "TemplateName": "prod-config-v1", "DateUpdated": "2024-01-15T10:35:00Z" }
# Add env var from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"CACHE_TTL=3600 added")
Example 4: Remove Option
#!/bin/bash
# Remove deprecated setting
aws elasticbeanstalk update-configuration-template \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --template-name prod-config-v1 \
  --options-to-remove \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=LEGACY_MODE
{ "TemplateName": "prod-config-v1", "DateUpdated": "2024-01-15T10:40:00Z" }
# Remove option from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"LEGACY_MODE removed")
Example 5: Update Description
#!/bin/bash
# Update template metadata
aws elasticbeanstalk update-configuration-template \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --template-name prod-config-v1 \
  --description "Production config v1.2 - Updated autoscaling and cache settings"

echo "✓ Template description updated"
✓ Template description updated
# Update desc from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Description updated")

delete-configuration-template

Permanently removes a configuration template from an application. This action cannot be undone.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk delete-configuration-template \
  --application-name  \
  --template-name  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string): [Required] Application name
  • --template-name (string): [Required] Template to delete
Return Value
  • None: Operation succeeds silently
Example 1: Delete Old Template
#!/bin/bash
# Delete deprecated template
aws elasticbeanstalk delete-configuration-template \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --template-name old-config-v1

echo "✓ Template deleted"
✓ Template deleted
# Delete template from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Old template removed")
Example 2: Delete with Confirmation
#!/bin/bash
# Delete with safety check
TEMPLATE="dev-test-config"
read -p "Delete template $TEMPLATE? (y/n): " confirm
if [ "$confirm" = "y" ]; then
  aws elasticbeanstalk delete-configuration-template \
    --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
    --template-name $TEMPLATE
  echo "✓ Template deleted"
fi
Delete template dev-test-config? (y/n): y ✓ Template deleted
# Confirm delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Confirmed and deleted")
Example 3: Cleanup Old Templates
#!/bin/bash
# Delete templates older than pattern
for template in old-v1 old-v2 deprecated-config; do
  aws elasticbeanstalk delete-configuration-template \
    --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
    --template-name $template 2>/dev/null && \
  echo "Deleted: $template"
done
Deleted: old-v1 Deleted: old-v2 Deleted: deprecated-config
# Cleanup batch from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"3 templates cleaned up")
Example 4: Delete Before Recreate
#!/bin/bash
# Delete and recreate template
aws elasticbeanstalk delete-configuration-template \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --template-name prod-config-v1 2>/dev/null

aws elasticbeanstalk create-configuration-template \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --template-name prod-config-v1 \
  --environment-id $ENV_ID

echo "✓ Template recreated fresh"
✓ Template recreated fresh
# Recreate from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Template recreated")
Example 5: Check Before Delete
#!/bin/bash
# Verify template exists, then delete
TEMPLATE="staging-config"
if aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-settings \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --template-name $TEMPLATE &>/dev/null; then
  aws elasticbeanstalk delete-configuration-template \
    --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
    --template-name $TEMPLATE
  echo "✓ Template $TEMPLATE deleted"
else
  echo "Template $TEMPLATE not found"
fi
✓ Template staging-config deleted
# Safe delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Safely removed template")

validate-configuration-settings

Validates configuration settings before applying them to an environment. Checks for errors and warnings to prevent deployment failures.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
  --application-name  \
  [--template-name ] \
  [--environment-name ] \
  --option-settings  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string): [Required] Application name
  • --template-name (string): Template context
  • --environment-name (string): Environment context
  • --option-settings (list): [Required] Settings to validate
Return Value
  • Messages (Array): Validation messages with severity
Example 1: Validate Instance Type
#!/bin/bash
# Validate before applying
aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.large
{ "Messages": [] }
# Validate ok from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Settings valid - no errors")
Example 2: Detect Invalid Value
#!/bin/bash
# Test invalid instance type
aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.fake
{ "Messages": [ { "Message": "Invalid value for InstanceType: t3.fake", "Severity": "error", "Namespace": "aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration", "OptionName": "InstanceType" } ] }
# Invalid value from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"ERROR: Invalid instance type")
Example 3: Validate Multiple Settings
#!/bin/bash
# Validate batch of settings
aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MinSize,Value=2 \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MaxSize,Value=10 \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.medium
{ "Messages": [] }
# Batch validate from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All settings valid")
Example 4: Check Template Settings
#!/bin/bash
# Validate against template
aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --template-name prod-config-v1 \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=DATABASE_URL,Value=postgres://localhost/db
{ "Messages": [ { "Message": "Value for DATABASE_URL should start with postgres://", "Severity": "warning" } ] }
# Template validate from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Warning: Check DB URL format")
Example 5: Validation Script
#!/bin/bash
# Validate and only apply if valid
RESULT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --environment-name anihpj-prod \
  --option-settings \
    Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.large \
  --query 'Messages[?Severity==`error`]' \
  --output text)

if [ -z "$RESULT" ]; then
  echo "✓ Validation passed - applying..."
  aws elasticbeanstalk update-environment --environment-name anihpj-prod --option-settings ...
else
  echo "✗ Validation failed: $RESULT"
fi
✓ Validation passed - applying...
# Safe apply from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Validated then applied")

list-available-solution-stacks

Returns a list of available platform versions (solution stacks) that can be used to create environments. Use to find Python/Django-compatible platforms.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --region (string): AWS region
Return Value
  • SolutionStacks (Array): List of platform names
  • SolutionStackDetails (Array): Detailed platform info
Example 1: List Python Platforms
#!/bin/bash
# Find Python platforms for Django
aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
  --query "SolutionStacks[?contains(@, 'Python')]" \
  --output table
[ "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12", "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.11", "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.9", "64bit Amazon Linux 2 v3.5.0 running Python 3.8" ]
# Python platforms from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"4 Python versions available")
Example 2: Get Latest Python 3.12
#!/bin/bash
# Get latest Python 3.12 stack
STACK=$(aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
  --query "SolutionStacks[?contains(@, 'Python 3.12')] | [0]" \
  --output text)

echo "Latest Python 3.12: $STACK"
Latest Python 3.12: 64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12
# Latest Python from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Use Python 3.12 for Django")
Example 3: List All Docker Platforms
#!/bin/bash
# Find Docker platforms
aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
  --query "SolutionStacks[?contains(@, 'Docker')]"
[ "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Docker", "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running ECS", "64bit Amazon Linux 2 v3.5.0 running Docker" ]
# Docker platforms from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Docker options for containerized Django")
Example 4: List Amazon Linux 2023 Stacks
#!/bin/bash
# Get all AL2023 platforms
aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
  --query "SolutionStacks[?contains(@, 'Amazon Linux 2023')]" \
  --output text | tr '\t' '\n'
64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12 64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.11 64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Node.js 20 64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Docker
# AL2023 platforms from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Latest Amazon Linux options")
Example 5: Export All Stacks
#!/bin/bash
# Export full list for reference
aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
  --query 'SolutionStacks' \
  > available-platforms.json

echo "✓ $(cat available-platforms.json | wc -l) platforms exported"
✓ 45 platforms exported
# Export platforms from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All platforms saved")

check-dns-availability

Checks if a CNAME prefix is available for use when creating a new Elastic Beanstalk environment. The CNAME creates a subdomain under elasticbeanstalk.com.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk check-dns-availability \
  --cname-prefix  \
